title: Metro de Madrid
summary: 12 lines, 300+ stations, the fastest way around. Open 6am-1:30am (2:30am Fri/Sat). €1.50-2.00 single, €12.20 for 10 trips.
content: Metro de Madrid is one of the world's best metro systems — 12 lines, 300+ stations, 1,700+ trains. Clean, fast, frequent (every 2-5 min peak). Open 6am-1:30am Sun-Wed, 6am-2:30am Fri-Sat. FARES: Single €1.50-2.00 (zone dependent). 10-trip Metrobús €12.20 (works on metro + EMT buses). Monthly pass (Abono) €54.60 zone A. Tourist pass 1 day €8.40, 3 days €17. Airport supplement €4.50 (Line 8). TICKETS: Buy at station machines (English option) or use Tarjeta Transporte Público (rechargeable card, €2.50 deposit). The 10-trip Metrobús is the best value for tourists. APPS: Metro de Madrid app (English) — route planner, real-time arrivals, service alerts, offline map. KEY STATIONS: Sol (Lines 1,2,3 — center), Atocha Renfe (Line 1 — trains), Chamartín (Lines 1,10 — north station), Aeropuerto (Line 8 — airport), Nuevos Ministerios (Lines 6,8,10 — hub). TIPS: (1) Avoid rush hour 8-10am and 6-8pm. (2) Keep your ticket until you exit — inspections are frequent and fines are €50. (3) Summer 2026: closures on Lines 6, 7, 9B, 10 — free shuttle buses. (4) Line 8 to the airport has a €4.50 supplement. (5) Elevators at most major stations — check the app for accessibility. (6) The Metro is safe but watch for pickpockets on Lines 1 and 5 (tourist routes).

sections.heading: Summer 2026 Closures
sections.body: Planned closures on Lines 6, 7, 9B, and 10 for renovations. Free shuttle buses cover affected sections. Check the Metro de Madrid app before traveling.

sections.heading: Metro Etiquette
do: Keep your ticket until you exit
Move to the center of the car

title: Adolfo Suárez Madrid-Barajas Airport (MAD)
summary: Spain's busiest airport, 12km NE of city. 4 terminals + T4S satellite. Metro L8, Express Bus 203, taxi €30 flat rate.
content: Adolfo Suárez Madrid-Barajas Airport (MAD) is Spain's busiest airport, 12km northeast of Madrid city center. 4 terminals: T1, T2, T3 (older, connected) and T4 + T4S satellite (modern, Iberia and Oneworld alliance). FREE shuttle between terminals (5-10 min, every 5 min). TO/FROM CITY CENTER: (1) Metro Line 8 (pink): all terminals to Nuevos Ministerios (15 min, €4.50 airport supplement + €1.50-3.00 metro fare). Run 6am-1:30am. (2) Express Bus 203: 24/7 to Atocha (daytime) / Cibeles (nighttime). €5, 30-40 min. (3) Taxi: €30 flat rate to central Madrid (inside M-30 ring). 20-30 min. (4) Ride-sharing (Uber/Cabify): €25-35, 20-30 min. Pick-up at 'Ride App' zones. (5) Cercanías C1: from T4 to Atocha/Chamartín (25 min, €2.60). Only useful if arriving at T4. (6) Private transfer: €35-60, pre-booked. AIRPORT FACILITIES: Free WiFi (AENA WiFi, 30 min free then paid), ATMs (use bank ATMs not Euronet), currency exchange (poor rates — use ATMs), left luggage (T1 €4/bag/day, T4 €5.50/bag/day, 24/7), duty-free, car rental (all major companies at T1, T2, T4), VIP lounges, pharmacy (T2, T4), medical center (T2). TIPS: (1) Arrive 2 hours before European flights, 3 hours for long-haul. (2) T4 is the best terminal — modern, good food, shopping. T1-T3 are older and more crowded. (3) The Metro is the cheapest option but can be tricky with large luggage during rush hour. (4) The €30 taxi flat rate is often the best value for groups. (5) Free WiFi is limited to 30 min — buy more or use your eSIM data.

sections.heading: Airport to City Center — Transport Options
Which terminal?
sections.body: Iberia and Oneworld partners use T4/T4S. Most other airlines use T1/T2/T3. Check your boarding pass — T4 to T1 is a 20-min shuttle ride. Allow extra time if transferring between terminals.

title: Atocha Station (Madrid's Main Train Station)
summary: Madrid's main station. AVE high-speed to Barcelona, Seville, Valencia. Tropical garden in the old hall. Metro: Atocha Renfe L1.
content: Atocha Station (officially Madrid Puerta de Atocha) is Madrid's main train station, serving high-speed AVE trains and most long-distance routes. Two parts: (1) Atocha Cercanías — commuter trains (Cercanías C1-C10), in the modern part. (2) Madrid Puerta de Atocha — long-distance and AVE high-speed trains, in the restored 19th-century hall with the famous tropical garden (4,000 plants, turtles). AVE DESTINATIONS: Barcelona (2h30, from €25), Seville (2h30, from €20), Valencia (1h40, from €18), Málaga (2h30, from €25), Zaragoza (1h15, from €15), Córdoba (1h45, from €18). Metro: Atocha Renfe (Line 1), Atocha (Line 1, walk 5 min). Cercanías: connects to Chamartín (8 min, faster than Metro), Airport T4 (25 min, C1), El Escorial (1h, C8). FACILITIES: Left luggage (€3.90-5.50/day, ground floor near tropical garden), VIPS café, Starbucks, McDonald's, pharmacy, car rental, taxi rank, hotel booking desk, tropical garden (free to visit, even without a ticket). TIPS: (1) The tropical garden in the old hall is worth seeing even if you're not catching a train — 4,000 plants, turtles, free. (2) Arrive 30 min before AVE departure — security check is like an airport. (3) AVE tickets are cheapest 60+ days ahead — book at renfe.com. (4) Left luggage at Atocha is the cheapest in central Madrid (€3.90/day) — great for sightseeing. (5) The station is 10 min walk from the Prado, Reina Sofía, and Thyssen museums.

sections.heading: Tropical Garden
sections.body: The old 19th-century hall is now a 4,000-plant tropical garden with turtles. Free to visit — worth a 10-minute wander even if you're not catching a train.

title: Chamartín Station (Madrid's North Station)
summary: Madrid's second major station, north of center. AVE to Galicia, Asturias, Basque Country. Cercanías hub. Metro: Lines 1, 10.
content: Chamartín Station (officially Madrid-Chamartín-Clara Campoamor) is Madrid's second major train station, located in the north of the city. Renovated in 2022 with a modern glass facade and expanded concourse. AVE high-speed trains depart here for: Galicia (A Coruña, Vigo — 6h), Asturias (Gijón, Oviedo — 4h30), Cantabria (Santander — 4h15), Basque Country (Bilbao — 4h, San Sebastián — 5h30). Also serves Cercanías lines C1, C2, C3, C4, C7, C8, C10 — connecting to Atocha (8 min), Barajas Airport T4 (25 min), El Escorial (1h), and Segovia (via C8 + C9). Metro: Chamartín (Lines 1, 10), plus direct connection to Metro surロン (Line 10). Facilities: VIPS café, Starbucks, McDonald's, pharmacy, car rental (Avis, Hertz), left luggage (€3.90/day), taxi rank, parking. The station is less crowded than Atocha and easier to navigate. For tourists staying in northern Madrid (Chamartín, Tetuán, financial district), this is more convenient than Atocha.

sections.heading: Less crowded than Atocha
sections.body: Chamartín is easier to navigate and less crowded than Atocha. For tourists staying in northern Madrid (Chamartín, Tetuán, financial district), this is more convenient. Connects to Atocha in 8 min via Cercanías C1.

title: EMT Madrid Buses (City Bus Network)
summary: 200+ lines across Madrid. €1.50 single, €1.20 with Tarjeta Transporte. Airport Express 203 (€5). Night buses (Búhos) from Cibeles.
content: EMT (Empresa Municipal de Transportes) operates Madrid's city bus network — 200+ lines, 2,000+ buses. Single ticket €1.50 (buy from driver, exact change appreciated). With Tarjeta Transporte Público: €1.20 per trip. 10-trip Metrobús card: €12.20 (works on Metro + buses). KEY LINES FOR TOURISTS: (1) Line 1, 2, 46 — north-south through center. (2) Line 27, 34, 45 — to museums (Prado, Reina Sofía). (3) Line 74 — to El Rastro flea market (Sundays). (4) Line 200, 203 — Airport Express (€5, 24/7). (5) Night buses N1-N28 — from Plaza de Cibeles after Metro closes. HOW TO USE: (1) Wait at a bus stop with a red/white sign showing the line number. (2) Flag the bus (raise your hand). (3) Enter through the front door. (4) Pay driver (cash) or tap your Tarjeta Transporte. (5) Press the 'stop' button before your stop. (6) Exit through the rear door. APP: Download 'EMT Madrid' app (English) — real-time bus arrivals, route planner, buy 10-trip tickets. Bus stops have QR codes — scan with your phone for real-time arrivals. TIPS: (1) Buses are slower than Metro for most routes but go where Metro doesn't. (2) Best for: cross-town routes, reaching neighborhoods not on Metro, airport. (3) Air-conditioned in summer. (4) wheelchair accessible (all buses have ramps). (5) The 10-trip Metrobús card is the best value — works on Metro AND buses.

title: Taxis in Madrid
summary: White taxis with red diagonal stripe. €2.40 initial fare. €30 flat rate airport to center. Book via PideTaxi app. Honest, metered.
content: Madrid taxis are white with a red diagonal stripe and the Madrid bear crest. They're honest, metered, and reliable — scams are rare. FARES: Initial fare €2.40 (daytime). €1.10/km city, €1.30/km outside city or at night. Airport supplement €5.50. Train/bus station supplement €2.95. Waiting time €22/hour. TYPICAL FARES: Sol to Retiro €8-10. Sol to Bernabéu €12-15. Center to airport €30 (flat rate inside M-30). Center to IFEMA €25-30. FLAT RATES (mandated by city): Airport to central Madrid (inside M-30) = €30 flat rate. Airport to IFEMA = €20 flat rate. These are displayed in the taxi. BOOKING: (1) Street hail — wave your hand, look for the green 'libre' light on the roof. (2) Taxi ranks — at all major plazas, stations, hotels. (3) PideTaxi app (official, English, same fares as street hail). (4) FreeNow app (international, same fares). (5) Phone: Radio Taxi 91 447 51 84. TIPS: (1) Always use the meter (taxímetro) — refuse fixed prices except airport flat rate. (2) Tip: round up to nearest euro, or €1-2 for good service. (3) 4 passengers max per taxi. (4) Large luggage in trunk (free). (5) Taxis can use bus lanes — faster than driving. (6) Night rate applies 11pm-6am and on holidays. (7) For airport: the €30 flat rate is usually cheaper than the meter.

sections.heading: Always use the meter
sections.body: Refuse fixed prices except the airport flat rate (€30 inside M-30, displayed in the taxi). Madrid taxis are honest — scams are rare. Tip: round up to the nearest euro.

title: Left Luggage (Consignas) — All Stations
summary: Left luggage facilities at Madrid's main transit hubs: Atocha, Chamartín, Barajas Airport, Méndez Álvaro bus station. €3-6/day.
content: Left luggage (consignas) facilities in Madrid are located at the main transit hubs. This is the master record — see related records for location-specific details. LOCATIONS: (1) Atocha Station — consignas on the ground floor near the tropical garden, €3.90/day per bag (size S/M), €5.50/day (size L). Open 5:30am-10:30pm daily. (2) Chamartín Station — consignas near the VIPS café, same pricing. Open 6am-11pm. (3) Barajas Airport — left luggage at T1 (4€/bag/day) and T4 (5.50€/bag/day), open 24/7. (4) Méndez Álvaro Bus Station (Estación Sur) — left luggage, €3/day. Open 6am-midnight. PRICES: €3-6 per bag per day depending on size and location. Pay by cash or card. SECURITY: all facilities are staffed and CCTV-monitored. Bags are X-rayed. ID required (passport). Tips: (1) Don't leave valuables, electronics, or important documents in left luggage. (2) Take a photo of your bag before leaving it. (3) Keep your claim ticket safe — losing it means a long ID verification process. (4) Some facilities have size limits (max 80cm). (5) For longer-term storage (weeks/months), see our Public Storage (Trasteros) record.

sections.heading: Left Luggage Locations & Prices
rows: Cheapest in center, near tropical garden

title: Renfe — National Trains
summary: Spain's national rail operator. AVE high-speed, Alvia, Cercanías commuter. renfe.com for tickets. Book 60+ days ahead for cheapest fares.
content: Renfe Operadora is Spain's national railway company. SERVICES: (1) AVE — high-speed (up to 310 km/h). Madrid to Barcelona 2h30, Seville 2h30, Valencia 1h40. From €15 if booked 60+ days ahead. (2) Alvia — medium-distance high-speed. Madrid to Galicia, Basque Country, Cantabria. (3) Cercanías — commuter rail in Madrid (9 lines, C1-C10). See our Cercanías record. (4) Larga Distancia — conventional long-distance (slower, cheaper). COMPETITORS: Ouigo (ouigo.com) and Iryo (iryo.eu) now compete with Renfe on the Madrid-Barcelona and Madrid-Valencia routes — often cheaper. BOOKING: renfe.com (English site available). Book 60+ days ahead for cheapest 'Promo' fares (€15-25). Last-minute tickets can be €150+. Tickets are e-tickets — show on your phone at the station. STATIONS: AVE trains depart from Atocha (most destinations) or Chamartín (Galicia, Asturias, Basque Country). Arrive 30 min before departure — security check like an airport. TIPS: (1) The 'Promo' fare is non-refundable but 50-70% cheaper. (2) Prenda-e (bike) service: €10 to bring a bike, book ahead. (3) Renfe+ loyalty program: free to join, accumulate points for discounts. (4) Cercanías is separate from AVE — different tickets, different platforms. (5) For Cercanías, use the Tarjeta Transporte Público (€2.50 deposit) — cheaper than single tickets.

sections.heading: Cheaper alternatives
sections.body: Ouigo (ouigo.com) and Iryo (iryo.eu) compete with Renfe on Madrid-Barcelona and Madrid-Valencia. Fares from €9. Same travel time, less legroom.

title: Schengen Visa — US Citizens
summary: US citizens: no visa needed for 90 days. Just bring a passport (3+ months validity). ETIAS coming Q4 2026 (€7, 3-year authorization).
content: Schengen visa rules for US citizens. THE GOOD NEWS: US citizens do NOT need a visa for Spain or any other Schengen country for stays up to 90 days within any 180-day period. Just show your US passport at the border. WHAT YOU NEED: (1) US passport valid for at least 3 months beyond your departure date. (2) No visa required for tourism, business meetings, or family visits up to 90 days. (3) Return/onward ticket (may be checked). (4) Proof of accommodation and funds (rarely checked for US citizens). WHAT YOU CAN'T DO: (1) Work in Spain (need a work visa). (2) Study for more than 90 days (need a student visa). (3) Stay more than 90 days in any 180-day period (need a long-stay visa). (4) Access Spanish public healthcare (need travel insurance). ETIAS (COMING Q4 2026): US citizens will need to apply for ETIAS (electronic authorization) before traveling to Spain. €7, valid 3 years. Takes 10 minutes online. Similar to the ESTA that foreign visitors need for the US. NOT a visa — just a pre-authorization. THE 90/180 RULE: You can stay 90 days within any rolling 180-day period. Example: if you enter on January 1 and stay 90 days, you can't re-enter until July 1 (180 days after January 1). Track your days carefully — overstaying can result in fines and a ban. TIPS: (1) Register with the US Embassy's STEP program before traveling. (2) The US Embassy in Madrid is at Calle de Serrano 75 — phone 91 587 2200. (3) US driver's licenses are valid in Spain for 90 days. (4) Your US health insurance likely doesn't cover you abroad — buy travel insurance. (5) No vaccinations required for Spain.

sections.heading: The 90/180 Rule
sections.body: You can stay 90 days within any rolling 180-day period across ALL Schengen countries. Example: enter Jan 1, stay 90 days, can't re-enter until July 1. Track your days carefully — overstaying can result in fines and a 1-3 year ban.

title: ETIAS — Coming Q4 2026
summary: ETIAS is Europe's new electronic travel authorization. Required for visa-exempt non-EU visitors (US, UK, Australia). Launch Q4 2026. €7, 3-year validity.
content: ETIAS (European Travel Information and Authorisation System) is Europe's new electronic travel authorization, similar to the US ESTA. It will be required for citizens of visa-exempt countries (US, UK, Canada, Australia, and 50+ others) visiting Spain and other Schengen countries. STATUS: Launch delayed multiple times. Currently expected Q4 2026. Check the official site (travel-europe.europa.eu/etias_en) for the latest. COST: €7 for ages 18-70, free for under 18 and over 70. Valid for 3 years or until passport expires. HOW TO APPLY: Online at the official ETIAS site — takes 10-15 minutes. You'll need: passport, email, credit/debit card. Most applications approved within minutes. WHO NEEDS IT: Citizens of the US, UK, Canada, Australia, New Zealand, Japan, and other visa-exempt countries. EU citizens do NOT need ETIAS. WHEN TO APPLY: At least 96 hours before your trip (though most are instant). WHAT IT DOES: Allows multiple entries into the Schengen area for up to 90 days per 180-day period. It's NOT a visa — it's a pre-travel authorization. TIPS: (1) Only apply through the official EU site (travel-europe.europa.eu/etias_en) — avoid scam sites that charge extra. (2) Your ETIAS is linked to your passport — if you get a new passport, you need a new ETIAS. (3) If denied, you can appeal. (4) ETIAS does not guarantee entry — border guards still make the final decision. (5) Until ETIAS launches, US/UK/Australian citizens can enter Spain visa-free with just a passport.

title: Schengen Visa — UK Citizens
summary: UK citizens: no visa needed for 90 days. Just bring a passport (3+ months validity). ETIAS coming Q4 2026 (€7, 3-year authorization).
content: Schengen visa rules for UK citizens after Brexit. THE GOOD NEWS: UK citizens do NOT need a visa for Spain or any other Schengen country for stays up to 90 days within any 180-day period. Just show your passport at the border. WHAT CHANGED WITH BREXIT: (1) UK citizens are now treated as third-country nationals (non-EU). (2) The 90/180-day rule applies — you can't stay more than 90 days in any 180-day period across all Schengen countries. (3) You may be asked to show a return ticket and proof of funds. (4) You can't work in Spain without a work visa. (5) Your passport must have at least 3 months validity beyond your departure date. (6) UK passports must have been issued within the last 10 years. ETIAS (COMING Q4 2026): UK citizens will need to apply for ETIAS (electronic authorization) before traveling. €7, valid 3 years. Takes 10 minutes online. NOT a visa — just a pre-authorization. FOR STAYS OVER 90 DAYS: You need a visa — student visa, work visa, or non-lucrative visa (retirees, remote workers). See our visa records. TIPS: (1) There are no border checks between Spain and Portugal or France (Schengen), but carry your passport. (2) The 90/180 rule is cumulative across all Schengen countries — track your days. (3) If you overstay, you can be fined and banned from Schengen for 1-3 years. (4) UK citizens can use the EU/EEA passport lanes at Spanish airports until ETIAS launches.

title: Passport Requirements
summary: Passport valid 3+ months beyond stay. No visa needed for 90 days (US, UK, Canada, Australia). Schengen rules apply. ETIAS coming Q4 2026.
content: Passport requirements for Spain. GENERAL RULE: Your passport must be valid for at least 3 months beyond your planned date of departure from the Schengen area. VISA-FREE ENTRY (90 days): Citizens of US, UK, Canada, Australia, New Zealand, Japan, and 50+ other countries can enter Spain without a visa for up to 90 days within any 180-day period. Just show your passport at border control. SCHENGEN RULES: Spain is part of the Schengen area (26 countries with no internal border checks). Your 90 days counts across ALL Schengen countries — you can't reset it by leaving and re-entering. ETIAS (COMING Q4 2026): New electronic authorization for visa-exempt visitors. €7, valid 3 years. See our ETIAS record. ENTRY REQUIREMENTS: Border guards may ask for: (1) Passport (3+ months validity). (2) Return/onward ticket. (3) Proof of accommodation (hotel booking, letter from host). (4) Proof of financial means (€100/day approx, bank statement or cash). (5) Travel insurance (recommended, not always checked). EXIT: No exit formalities for most nationalities. Keep your boarding pass as proof of departure. TIPS: (1) Make a photocopy of your passport and keep it separate from the original. (2) Take a photo of your passport and email it to yourself. (3) Leave your actual passport in the hotel safe — carry the copy for daily use. (4) If your passport is close to expiring, renew before traveling — the 3-month rule is strict. (5) Dual citizens: use the same passport for entry and exit.

title: Euros & ATMs in Madrid
summary: Euro (€). Use bank ATMs (Santander, BBVA, CaixaBank) for best rates. Avoid Euronet ATMs (high fees). Cash still needed for small shops.
content: Spain uses the Euro (€). ATM TIPS: (1) Use bank-affiliated ATMs (Santander, BBVA, CaixaBank, Bankinter, Sabadell) — they offer the interbank exchange rate with no markup. (2) AVOID Euronet and other non-bank ATMs — they charge 3-5% fees and offer a terrible 'dynamic currency conversion' rate. Always choose to be charged in EUR, not your home currency. (3) Withdraw larger amounts (€200-300) to minimize per-transaction fees. (4) Most ATMs accept 4-digit PINs. If your PIN is 6 digits, contact your bank before traveling. (5) Daily withdrawal limit: usually €500-600 from Spanish ATMs. CASH VS CARD: Spain is very card-friendly. Visa and Mastercard accepted almost everywhere. American Express less common. Contactless payment (Apple Pay, Google Pay) widely accepted. However, carry €50-100 cash for: small tapas bars, market stalls, taxis (some), tips, and emergencies. EXCHANGE: Avoid airport currency exchanges (worst rates). If you must exchange cash, use the banks on Gran Vía or El Corte Inglés. Better to just use ATMs. TIP: Notify your bank you're traveling to Spain before you go, to avoid fraud blocks.

sections.heading: ⚠️ Avoid Euronet ATMs
sections.body: Euronet and other non-bank ATMs charge 3–5% fees and offer terrible 'dynamic currency conversion' rates. Always choose to be charged in EUR, not your home currency. Use bank ATMs (Santander, BBVA, CaixaBank) instead.

title: Credit Cards & Contactless Payment
summary: Visa/Mastercard accepted almost everywhere. Amex less common. Contactless (Apple Pay, Google Pay) widely accepted.
content: Spain is very card-friendly. ACCEPTED: Visa and Mastercard are accepted at 95%+ of businesses. Contactless payment (Apple Pay, Google Pay, Samsung Pay) works at most terminals — just look for the contactless symbol. Tap to pay up to €50 without PIN. LESS COMMON: American Express — accepted at hotels, department stores, and larger restaurants, but NOT at small bars, taxis, or market stalls. Discover — rarely accepted. NOT ACCEPTED: Diners Club, JCB — essentially useless in Spain. WHEN YOU NEED CASH: (1) Small tapas bars and cafés (under €10). (2) Market stalls (El Rastro, Mercado de San Miguel). (3) Some taxis (though most now accept cards). (4) Tips (though rounding up on a card is fine). (5) Vending machines. (6) Public toilets (€0.50-1). TIPS: (1) Always choose to pay in EUR (not your home currency) when given the choice — 'dynamic currency conversion' is a scam with 3-5% markup. (2) Spanish PINs are 4 digits — if yours is 6, contact your bank. (3) Some restaurants bring a portable card terminal to your table — don't let your card out of sight. (4) Spanish banks don't charge you for using their ATMs, but YOUR bank might — check foreign transaction fees. (5) The best travel cards: Wise (0% foreign transaction fee), Revolut (0% on weekdays), Chase Sapphire (no foreign transaction fee).

title: Daily Budgets for Madrid
summary: Backpacker €40-60/day. Mid-range €100-180/day. Luxury €250+. Includes accommodation, food, transport, attractions.
content: Daily budgets for Madrid. BACKPACKER (€40-60/day): Hostel dorm €15-25, supermarket food €10, Metro €3, free attractions (Retiro, temples, plazas), 1 paid attraction €10. Total: ~€40-50/day. BUDGET TRAVELER (€60-100/day): Hostel private or budget hotel €35-50, menú del día €10-12, dinner tapas €10-15, Metro €5, attractions €15-20, drinks €5-10. Total: ~€80-100/day. MID-RANGE (€100-180/day): 3-star hotel €70-100, lunch menú €12, dinner restaurant €25-40, Metro/buses €5, attractions €20-30, drinks/coffee €10. Total: ~€140-180/day. COMFORTABLE (€180-300/day): 4-star hotel €100-180, lunch restaurant €20, dinner good restaurant €40-60, taxi €15, attractions €30, drinks €20. Total: ~€230-300/day. LUXURY (€300+/day): 5-star hotel €200-500, fine dining €80-150, private transfers €30, VIP attractions €50, cocktails €30. Total: €400+/day. SAVE MONEY: (1) Menú del día (3-course lunch €10-12, weekdays). (2) Free museum entry times (Prado Mon-Sat 6-8pm, etc.). (3) Metro 10-trip card (€12.20 = €1.22/trip vs €1.50-2.00 single). (4) Picnic in Retiro Park with supermarket food. (5) Free walking tours (tip €5-10). (6) Vermouth + tapas (€5-8) instead of restaurant lunch. SPEND MORE ON: (1) One great dinner (€40-80). (2) A flamenco show (€30-50). (3) A Bernabéu or Metropolitano stadium tour (€25-35). (4) A day trip to Toledo or Segovia (€15-25 train + €10-15 sights).

title: Survival Spanish — 20 Essential Phrases
summary: 20 phrases that will get you through any situation in Madrid. Print this and carry it. Free.
content: 20 essential Spanish phrases for Madrid. GREETINGS: (1) 'Hola, buenas' — Hello (casual, any time of day). (2) 'Buenos días' — Good morning. (3) 'Buenas tardes' — Good afternoon. (4) 'Buenas noches' — Good evening/night. (5) 'Adiós' / 'Hasta luego' — Goodbye / See you later. POLITE: (6) 'Por favor' — Please. (7) 'Gracias' — Thank you. (8) 'De nada' — You're welcome. (9) 'Perdone' — Excuse me / Sorry. (10) '¿Habla inglés?' — Do you speak English? RESTAURANT: (11) 'La carta, por favor' — The menu, please. (12) 'La cuenta, por favor' — The bill, please. (13) 'Para mí...' — For me... (ordering). (14) 'Sin gluten' — Gluten-free. DIRECTIONS: (15) '¿Dónde está...?' — Where is...? (16) '¿Dónde está el baño?' — Where is the bathroom? (17) '¿Cuánto cuesta?' — How much? NUMBERS: (18) 'Uno, dos, tres' — One, two, three. (19) 'Una cerveza, por favor' — One beer, please. EMERGENCY: (20) '¡Ayuda! Necesito un médico' — Help! I need a doctor. TIP: Download Google Translate, set Spanish offline, and use camera mode to translate menus and signs. Most Spaniards in tourist areas speak some English — but trying Spanish first goes a long way.

title: Emergency Numbers in Madrid
summary: 112 (free, multilingual, 24/7). Police 091, Ambulance 061, Fire 080. Works from any phone.
content: Emergency numbers in Madrid. 112 (EUROPEAN EMERGENCY NUMBER): Free, 24/7, multilingual (English, Spanish, French, German). Works from any phone (including locked phones, payphones, and without a SIM card). Use for: medical emergencies, fires, crime in progress, serious accidents. The operator will connect you to the right service. POLICE: 091 (Policía Nacional — national police, handles urban crime, immigration, terrorism). 092 (Policía Municipal — local/city police, handles traffic, noise complaints, neighborhood issues). AMBULANCE: 061 (ambulances, medical emergencies). FIRE: 080 (fire department, also rescues). OTHER USEFUL NUMBERS: 0116 (child protection), 016 (domestic violence — free, confidential, 24/7), 900 200 225 (gambling addiction). FOR NON-EMERGENCIES: File police reports online at denuncias.policia.es (works for non-residents, available in English). For theft without violence, this is faster than going to a police station. WHAT TO SAY IF YOU CALL 112: (1) 'I need an ambulance' / 'Necesito una ambulancia'. (2) 'I need the police' / 'Necesito la policía'. (3) State your location (nearest street, landmark, or Metro station). (4) Describe the emergency briefly. (5) Stay on the line until the operator tells you to hang up. TIPS: (1) Save 112 in your phone contacts. (2) 112 operators all speak English — don't panic. (3) If you can't speak, dial 112 and leave the line open — they can trace your location. (4) Spanish hospitals cannot refuse emergency treatment, regardless of insurance or residency status. (5) Carry your EHIC card (EU) or travel insurance info at all times.

sections.heading: 112 works from any phone
sections.body: Free. Multilingual (English, Spanish, French, German). Works from locked phones, payphones, and phones without a SIM card. The operator will connect you to the right service. Save 112 in your phone contacts.

sections.heading: What to Say When You Call 112

items.title: State the emergency type
items.body: 'I need an ambulance' / 'Necesito una ambulancia' or 'I need the police' / 'Necesito la policía'.

items.title: State your location
items.body: Nearest street, landmark, or Metro station. If you don't know, say 'I don't know my location' — they can trace your phone.

items.title: Describe the emergency briefly
items.body: Keep it simple. The operator speaks English.

items.title: Stay on the line
items.body: Don't hang up until the operator tells you to. They may need more information.

sections.heading: Non-emergency police report
sections.body: For theft without violence, file online at denuncias.policia.es (English available). Faster than going to a police station. Print the report — you'll need it for insurance and embassy.

title: Hospitals & 24h Clinics in Madrid
summary: [Archived: merged into another record. Same content as hospitals-madrid. Use hospitals-madrid.] Public: Gregorio Marañón, La Paz, 12 de Octubre. Private: Ruber Internacional, HM Montepríncipe. ER free for all.
content: Madrid hospitals. PUBLIC (free with Social Security, EHIC, or for emergencies — cannot refuse anyone): (1) Hospital Universitario Gregorio Marañón (Calle Doctor Esquerdo 46, Metro O'Donnell L2) — largest, all specialties, 24/7 ER. (2) Hospital Universitario La Paz (Paseo de la Castellana 261, Metro La Paz L7) — top trauma center, pediatric ER, burns unit. (3) Hospital Universitario 12 de Octubre (Calle de Andalucía, Metro San Bernardo L6) — largest in southern Madrid. (4) Hospital Clínico San Carlos (Calle Professor Martín Lagos, Metro Ciudad Universitaria L6) — central, near university. PRIVATE (insurance or self-pay, shorter waits): (1) Hospital Ruber Internacional (Calle La Masó 38, Mirasierra) — top private, English-speaking. ER €200-500 self-pay. (2) Hospital Universitario HM Montepríncipe (Calle Avivé, Boadilla del Monte) — modern, English-speaking. (3) Hospital San Rafael (Calle Julio Palacios, Metro Valdeacederas L1) — mid-range. (4) Hospital Vithas Madrid (multiple) — chain. EMERGENCY: 112 (ambulance, free). Public ER wait: 2-6 hours. Private ER wait: 30-60 min, €200-500. Bring: passport/ID, insurance card, medication list. Spanish hospitals are required by law to treat emergencies regardless of insurance or residency — don't hesitate to go if you need care.

title: Embassies & Consulates in Madrid
summary: US Embassy: Serrano 75. UK Consulate: Paseo de la Castellana. Most embassies in Salamanca district. 24/7 emergency for citizens.
content: Embassies and consulates in Madrid. Most are in the Salamanca and Chamberí districts. UNITED STATES: Embassy (Calle de Serrano 75, Metro Serrano L4). Phone 91 587 2200. Consular section: passport services, citizenship, notarials. Appointment required — schedule at usais.usmission.gov. Emergency (24/7): 91 587 2200 (ask for American Citizen Services). UNITÉD KINGDOM: Embassy (Calle de la Farfa 16, Metro Nuñez de Balboa L5/L9). Phone 91 714 6300. Consular services: passport, emergency travel documents. Emergency (24/7): 91 714 6300. CANADA: Embassy (Calle de Núñez de Balboa 35, Metro Nuñez de Balboa L5/L9). Phone 91 382 8400. AUSTRALIA: Embassy (Calle de la Planelles 10, Metro República Argentina L6). Phone 91 353 6600. IRELAND: Embassy (Calle de la Claudio Coello 73, Metro Rubén Darío L5). Phone 91 576 3500. WHAT EMBASSIES CAN DO: (1) Emergency passport replacement. (2) Notarials and document authentication. (3) Emergency financial assistance (loan). (4) Visit if arrested. (5) Death of a citizen abroad. WHAT THEY CAN'T DO: (1) Get you out of jail. (2) Pay fines or legal fees. (3) Provide money (except emergency loans). (4) Intervene in Spanish legal proceedings. TIPS: (1) Register your trip with your embassy (STEP for US, LOCATE for UK) before traveling. (2) Keep your embassy's phone number in your phone. (3) For lost/stolen passport: file a police report first (online at denuncias.policia.es), then contact your embassy. (4) Embassies are closed on Spanish AND their home country's holidays. (5) Most embassy websites have English emergency information.

sections.heading: What embassies CAN'T do
sections.body: Get you out of jail. Pay fines or legal fees. Provide money (except emergency loans). Intervene in Spanish legal proceedings. They CAN: replace lost passports, notarize documents, visit if arrested, help in emergencies.

items.title: File a police report
items.body: Online at denuncias.policia.es (English, works for non-residents). Print the report.

items.title: Contact your embassy
items.body: Book an appointment online. Bring: police report, passport photo, passport number (if you have a copy), fee (€100–150).

items.title: Get emergency passport
items.body: Usually ready in 1–2 business days. US: valid 1 year. UK: single journey. Canada: 1–3 days.

items.title: File travel insurance claim
items.body: Most travel insurance covers passport replacement. Keep all receipts and the police report.

title: Lost or Stolen Passport — What to Do
summary: 1. File police report online (denuncias.policia.es). 2. Contact your embassy. 3. Get emergency passport. Allow 1-3 days.
content: Lost or stolen passport in Madrid. STEP 1 — FILE A POLICE REPORT: File online at denuncias.policia.es (available in English, works for non-residents). Choose 'robo' (theft) or 'pérdida' (loss). For theft with violence, call 112 or go to a police station in person. Print the report — you'll need it for insurance and your embassy. STEP 2 — CONTACT YOUR EMBASSY: US Embassy (Serrano 75, 91 587 2200, appointment required at usais.usmission.gov). UK Embassy (Farfa 16, 91 714 6300). Canadian Embassy (Núñez de Balboa 35, 91 382 8400). Australian Embassy (Planelles 10, 91 353 6600). Bring: police report, passport photo (or they can take one), passport number (if you have a copy), travel itinerary, fee (€100-150 for emergency passport). STEP 3 — EMERGENCY PASSPORT: US: emergency passport in 1-2 business days, valid for 1 year. UK: emergency travel document in 1-2 business days, valid for single journey. Canada: emergency passport in 1-3 business days. STEP 4 — TRAVEL INSURANCE: Most travel insurance covers passport replacement costs. Keep all receipts and the police report for your claim. PREVENTION: (1) Leave your passport in the hotel safe — carry a photocopy. (2) Take photos of your passport and store in cloud. (3) Email yourself a copy. (4) Register with your embassy before traveling (STEP for US, LOCATE for UK). (5) Use a money belt in tourist areas. TIPS: (1) The police report is essential — don't skip this step. (2) Embassy appointments can be booked online — don't just show up. (3) If you have a flight in 24 hours, tell the embassy — they can expedite. (4) Emergency passports may not be accepted for all destinations — check with your airline.

items.title: File police report online
items.body: Go to denuncias.policia.es (English available). Choose 'robo' (theft) or 'pérdida' (loss). Print the report.
items.tip: For theft with violence, call 112 or go to a police station in person.

items.title: Contact your embassy
items.body: Book appointment online. Bring: police report, passport photo, passport number (if you have a copy), fee (€100-150).

items.title: Get emergency passport
items.body: US: 1-2 business days, valid 1 year. UK: 1-2 days, single journey. Canada: 1-3 days.

items.title: File insurance claim
items.body: Most travel insurance covers passport replacement. Keep all receipts and the police report.

title: Pharmacies (Farmacias) in Madrid
summary: Green cross sign. 24-hour 'farmacia de guardia' rotates daily. Free health advice for minor issues. Cheaper than doctors.
content: Pharmacies (farmacias) in Madrid. IDENTIFY: Look for a green cross sign (sometimes flashing) — that's a pharmacy. There are 800+ pharmacies in Madrid, one every few blocks. HOURS: Most pharmacies open Mon-Fri 9:30am-2pm and 5-9:30pm, Sat 10am-2pm. Closed Sundays. 24-HOUR PHARMACIES (Farmacia de Guardia): Every night and Sunday, one pharmacy in each district stays open 24/7 on a rotating schedule. Find the nearest at colegiofarmaceuticosmadrid.es (Spanish only) or ask any pharmacy '¿Dónde está la farmacia de guardia?' WHAT THEY SELL: (1) Over-the-counter medicine: paracetamol (€2), ibuprofen (€3), antihistamines (€5), antacids (€4), cough syrup (€5), antibiotic cream (€5). (2) Prescription medicine (bring your prescription or have a doctor call one in). (3) First aid: bandages, antiseptic, thermometer. (4) Personal care: sunscreen, contact lens solution, feminine hygiene, baby formula. (5) Medical devices: blood pressure monitors, glucose meters. SPANISH PHARMACY TIPS: (1) Spanish pharmacists are highly trained (5-year university degree) and can advise on minor health issues — colds, allergies, rashes, insect bites. This is FREE and often faster than seeing a doctor. (2) Many medicines that require a prescription in the US/UK are available OTC in Spain (e.g., some antibiotics, codeine). Ask the pharmacist. (3) Spanish pharmacies are cheaper than US/UK for most medicines. (4) Say 'para el dolor de...' (for pain of...) + cabeza (head), estómago (stomach), garganta (throat). (5) The pharmacist can tell you if you need to see a doctor. (6) Emergency contraception is available OTC (€20-30, no prescription, no age restriction).

items.label: Free advice
items.value: Pharmacists can advise on minor health issues

sections.heading: Free health advice
sections.body: Spanish pharmacists have a 5-year university degree and can advise on minor health issues — colds, allergies, rashes, insect bites. This is FREE and often faster than seeing a doctor. Say 'para el dolor de...' (for pain of...) + cabeza (head), estómago (stomach), garganta (throat).

items.title: Search online
items.body: Go to colegiofarmaceuticosmadrid.es (Spanish only) for the daily 24h pharmacy list.

items.title: Ask any pharmacy
items.body: Walk into any pharmacy and ask '¿Dónde está la farmacia de guardia?' (Where is the on-duty pharmacy?).

items.title: Look for the sign
items.body: Closed pharmacies post the nearest 24h pharmacy address on their door.

title: NIE / TIE — Foreigner ID
summary: NIE is the foreigner ID number; TIE is the physical card. Required for everything official.
content: NIE (Número de Identidad de Extranjero): apply at any Oficina de Extranjería with passport, EX-15 form, reason for needing it (job, studies, property), €10.80 fee. TIE (Tarjeta de Identidad de Extranjero): physical card for non-EU residents with a permit of 6+ months. Apply at the police station (Comisaría) on Calle Silva or Montera after your residence permit is approved. Both require appointments — book weeks ahead.

sections.heading: What is a NIE?
sections.body: The NIE (Número de Identidad de Extranjero) is a unique identification number assigned to all foreigners in Spain. It's required for almost everything: opening bank accounts, signing rental contracts, working, buying property. You cannot live or work legally in Spain without one — it should be your first priority upon arrival.

items.title: EX-15 Form (completed and signed)
items.body: Download from the Spanish government website or pick up at the police station.

items.title: Original Passport + Photocopy
items.body: All pages including stamps and visas.

items.title: Reason for NIE (justification)
items.body: Work contract, university enrollment, property purchase, etc.

items.title: Tax Payment Receipt (Modelo 790)
items.body: Pay €9.64 at any Spanish bank before your appointment.

items.title: Book an Appointment
items.body: Visit sede.administracionespublicas.gob.es and book an appointment for 'NIE' at your local police station.
items.tip: Appointments fill up quickly. Check daily at 9 AM when new slots are released.

items.title: Prepare Documents
items.body: Download and complete the EX-15 form. Gather all required documents and make photocopies.

items.title: Pay the Fee
items.body: Pay €9.64 at any Spanish bank using the Modelo 790 form. Keep the stamped receipt as proof of payment.

items.title: Attend Your Appointment
items.body: Arrive 15 minutes early with all documents. The process usually takes 30–60 minutes if everything is in order.

items.title: Receive Your NIE
items.body: If approved, you'll receive your NIE certificate immediately. Make several photocopies — you'll need them for everything!

title: Empadronamiento
summary: [Archived: merged into another record. Same procedure as padron-empadronamiento (Empadronamiento is the verb, Padrón Municipal is the noun). Use padron-empadronamiento.] Required local registration. Free. Bring passport + proof of address to any Citizen Service Office. Needed for healthcare, schools, voting.
content: The Empadronamiento (also called Padrón Municipal) is Madrid's local resident register. It's MANDATORY for ALL Madrid residents — Spanish, EU, non-EU, everyone. It's FREE and easy. WHAT IT'S NEEDED FOR: (1) Public healthcare access. (2) School enrollment for children. (3) Voting in municipal elections (EU citizens). (4) Social services (subsidized housing, school meals). (5) Some bureaucratic procedures (NIE, TIE). HOW TO REGISTER: (1) Book an appointment at cita-previa.madrid.es (or walk in, but waits are long). (2) Go to any Oficina de Atención al Ciudadano (Citizen Service Office) — there are 25+ across Madrid. (3) Bring: passport (and NIE if you have one), proof of address (rental contract, utility bill in your name, or a signed letter from the person you live with + their ID + their empadronamiento). (4) Fill out the form (Spanish — bring a friend or use Google Translate). (5) Receive your volante de empadronamiento (certificate) immediately. TIPS: (1) It's free. (2) Takes 10-15 minutes if you have an appointment. (3) The certificate (volante) is valid for 3 months — request a new one when needed. (4) Update your empadronamiento when you move. (5) If you live in a shared flat and aren't on the lease, the leaseholder must sign a letter authorizing your registration at that address. (6) Some offices have English-speaking staff — call ahead to check. (7) You can also do it online if you have a digital certificate (certificado digital).

items.title: Passport (and NIE if you have one)
items.body: Original + photocopy

items.title: Proof of address
items.body: Rental contract, utility bill in your name, OR signed letter from leaseholder + their ID + their empadronamiento

items.title: Appointment confirmation
items.body: Book at cita-previa.madrid.es (or walk in, but expect long waits)

sections.heading: Why you need this
sections.body: Without empadronamiento, you can't access public healthcare, enroll children in school, vote in municipal elections, or access most social services. It's free, easy, and mandatory for all Madrid residents.

title: Idealista — Apartment Search
summary: Spain's #1 property portal. Search apartments, rooms, offices. English version available. Free to search.
content: Idealista (idealista.com) is Spain's largest property portal — the go-to site for finding apartments in Madrid. It lists properties for rent and sale, rooms in shared flats, and shared offices. HOW TO USE: (1) Go to idealista.com (English version available). (2) Search by neighborhood (barrio), price range, size, bedrooms. (3) Filter by: furnished (amueblado), pets (admite mascotas), elevator (ascensor), parking (garaje). (4) Contact landlord or agent directly through the site. (5) Save searches and get email alerts for new listings. ADVANTAGES: (1) Largest inventory — 300,000+ listings in Madrid. (2) English version available. (3) Map search is excellent. (4) Price/m² shown for each listing — helps spot overpriced apartments. (5) 'Verified' badge for some listings (landlord ID confirmed). (6) Neighborhood guides and price data. DISADVANTAGES: (1) Competition is fierce — good apartments get 10+ inquiries in the first hour. (2) Some listings are outdated. (3) Agents charge 1 month's rent + VAT as a fee (inmobiliaria fee). (4) Scam listings exist — never wire money before visiting. TIPS: (1) Set up email alerts — you need to be among the first to inquire. (2) Have your documents ready (passport, NIE, proof of income, bank statement). (3) Prepare a brief intro message in Spanish. (4) Visit apartments quickly — don't wait more than 1-2 days. (5) The best time to search: end of month (people moving out) and September (new rental season). (6) Cross-reference with Fotocasa, Badi (rooms), and Spotahome (verified apartments).

sections.heading: Act fast
sections.body: Good apartments get 10+ inquiries in the first hour. Set up email alerts, have your documents ready (passport, NIE, proof of income), and prepare a brief intro message in Spanish. Visit apartments within 24-48 hours of seeing the listing.

title: Fotocasa — Property Portal
summary: Spain's #2 property portal. Search apartments, rooms, offices. Filter by neighborhood, price, size. Free to search.
content: Fotocasa (fotocasa.es) is Spain's second-largest property portal, alongside Idealista. It lists apartments for rent and sale, rooms in shared flats, and commercial property. HOW TO USE: (1) Go to fotocasa.es (Spanish only — use Google Translate). (2) Search by neighborhood, price range, size, number of bedrooms. (3) Filter by: rent (alquiler) or buy (compra), furnished (amueblado), pets allowed (admite mascotas). (4) Contact the landlord or agent directly through the site. ADVANTAGES: (1) Large inventory — 200,000+ listings in Madrid. (2) Map view is excellent — see exactly where the apartment is. (3) Price history for some listings (shows if the price has been reduced). (4) 'Report' button for scam listings. DISADVANTAGES: (1) Spanish only — no English version. (2) Some listings are outdated (already rented but still showing). (3) Agents post duplicate listings. (4) No verification of listings (scams possible). TIPS: (1) Check both Fotocasa AND Idealista — they have different inventory. (2) Cross-reference listings on Badi (for rooms) and Spotahome (for verified apartments). (3) Never wire money before visiting the apartment. (4) Beware of listings that seem too cheap — they're almost always scams. (5) Contact multiple landlords — response rates are low (10-30%). (6) The best apartments are gone within 24-48 hours — check daily and act fast.

title: Madrid Neighborhoods Guide
summary: Overview of Madrid's 10 main neighborhoods for visitors and expats. Each has its own character — pick 2-3 to explore per day.
content: Madrid Neighborhoods Guide — overview of the 10 main areas for visitors and expats. CENTRAL: (1) Sol & Gran Vía — tourist center, shopping, theaters. Best for first-time visitors. Noisy, busy, overpriced restaurants. (2) Malasaña — hipster, vintage, craft beer, alternative nightlife. Best for 20s-30s travelers. (3) Chueca — LGBTQ+, chic boutiques, cocktail bars. Best for shopping and nightlife. HISTORIC: (4) La Latina — tapas, El Rastro flea market, traditional vermouth. Best for food lovers. (5) Lavapiés — multicultural, Indian curry, flamenco, alternative arts. Best for adventurous eaters. RESIDENTIAL: (6) Chamberí — authentic, local, calm. Best for a quieter base. (7) Retiro — parks, museums, upscale. Best for museum-goers. UPSCALE: (8) Salamanca — luxury shopping, embassies, fine dining. Best for luxury travelers. STUDENT & WEST: (9) Argüelles & Moncloa — students, parks, Temple of Debod sunset. Best for budget travelers. NORTH: (10) Tetuán & Cuatro Caminos — diverse, working-class, cheap eats. Best for budget long-term stays. HOW TO CHOOSE WHERE TO STAY: (1) First visit: Sol/Gran Vía or Chamberí. (2) Nightlife: Malasaña or Chueca. (3) Food: La Latina. (4) Museums: Retiro. (5) Budget: Argüelles or Tetuán. (6) Luxury: Salamanca. (7) Authentic/local: Chamberí or La Latina. EXPLORING: Each neighborhood takes 2-4 hours to walk. Best approach: Sol → Plaza Mayor → La Latina (morning tapas), then Malasaña → Chueca (afternoon vintage), then Gran Vía (evening). See our individual neighborhood records for detailed guides.

title: Rental Contracts & Deposits
summary: Standard lease: 5 years (landlord) or 7 years (company). Deposit: 2 months' rent. Eviction takes 6-12 months. Get everything in writing.
content: Rental contracts in Madrid (Ley de Arrendamientos Urbanos, LAU). STANDARD LEASE TERMS: (1) Duration: minimum 5 years for individual landlords, 7 years for companies. (2) After the initial term, it auto-renews in 1-year increments up to 3 more years (unless landlord needs to move in). (3) Deposit (fianza): 2 months' rent for long-term rentals, 1 month for rooms. Held by the Comunidad de Madrid. (4) Rent: paid monthly, usually by direct debit (domiciliación bancaria). (5) Utilities (luz, agua, gas, internet): paid by tenant, €50-150/month. (6) IBI (property tax): paid by landlord. (7) Community fees (gastos de comunidad): usually paid by landlord, but sometimes by tenant — check the contract. YOUR RIGHTS: (1) The landlord can't evict you during the lease term (unless you don't pay rent or damage the property). (2) The landlord can't raise the rent during the lease term (except by CPI inflation, capped at 2% for 2024). (3) You can leave with 30 days' notice after 6 months. (4) The landlord must give 4 months' notice if they want the apartment back at the end of the lease (for personal use). YOUR OBLIGATIONS: (1) Pay rent on time. (2) Keep the apartment in good condition. (3) Pay for minor repairs (major structural repairs are the landlord's responsibility). (4) Give 30 days' notice before leaving. TIPS: (1) Get EVERYTHING in writing — verbal agreements are worthless. (2) Never pay cash — use bank transfer (proves payment). (3) Take photos of the apartment's condition when you move in and out. (4) The deposit return can take 1-3 months after you leave — follow up with the Comunidad de Madrid. (5) If the landlord withholds the deposit unfairly, you can sue (small claims, 6-12 months). (6) For room rentals (not full apartments), the LAU may not apply — the contract is more flexible but less protected.

sections.heading: Get everything in writing
sections.body: Verbal agreements are worthless in Spain. Never pay cash — use bank transfers (they prove payment). Take photos of the apartment's condition when you move in and out. The deposit return can take 1-3 months — follow up with the Comunidad de Madrid.

title: Spanish Bank Accounts
summary: [Archived: merged into another record. Same topic as banking. Use banking as the canonical record.] Open a 'cuenta no residente' with just a passport. Major banks: BBVA, Santander, CaixaBank. €15-30/quarter fee, often waivable.
content: Opening a Spanish bank account. FOR NON-RESIDENTS (no NIE yet): You can open a 'cuenta no residente' (non-resident account) with just your passport. Major banks: BBVA, Santander, CaixaBank, Bankinter, Sabadell. Process: (1) Go to a branch with your passport. (2) Fill out a form (Spanish — bring a friend or use Google Translate). (3) Deposit a minimum amount (€50-150). (4) Receive your debit card and online banking credentials in 1-2 weeks. FEES: €15-30/quarter account maintenance. Usually waivable if you deposit €600+/month (e.g., salary) or maintain a minimum balance. Ask about 'cuenta sin comisiones' (no-fee account). FOR RESIDENTS (with NIE): Same process but you provide your NIE instead of passport. Lower fees, more services. ONLINE/DIGITAL BANKS: N26 (German, Spanish IBAN, English app, free account). Revolut (UK-based, Spanish IBAN available, free basic). Bunq (Dutch, premium from €3/month). These are faster to open (10 min online) and have no fees, but no physical branches. WHAT YOU GET: Spanish IBAN (ES + 22 digits), debit card (Visa/Mastercard), online banking (app + website), ability to set up direct debits (domiciliación bancaria) for rent, utilities, phone. CREDIT CARDS: Harder to get as a non-resident — usually require 6+ months of account history. Start with your debit card and apply later. TIPS: (1) BBVA and Santander have the most English-speaking staff. (2) Always ask for 'sin comisiones' (no fees) — they often can waive them. (3) Spanish banks require a Spanish phone number for 2FA. (4) Avoid taking cash out from non-bank ATMs (Euronet) — 3-5% fees.

sections.heading: Ask for 'sin comisiones'
sections.body: Always ask for 'sin comisiones' (no fees) — banks often waive the quarterly fee if you deposit €600+/month or maintain a minimum balance.

title: Public Healthcare (Seguridad Social)
summary: Free for residents with Social Security. EU citizens use EHIC. Non-EU need private insurance (€40-80/month). Quality is good.
content: Spain's public healthcare system (Seguridad Social) is one of the best in the world — ranked #7 globally by WHO. WHO GETS IT FREE: (1) Spanish citizens. (2) Legal residents registered with Social Security (employed or self-employed). (3) EU citizens with an EHIC card (covers temporary stays). (4) Pensioners from EU countries with an S1 form. (5) Pregnant women and children under 18 (regardless of status). HOW TO ACCESS: (1) Get your NIE (foreigner ID). (2) Register with Social Security (get a número de Seguridad Social). (3) Get your empadronamiento (local registration). (4) Apply for a health card (tarjeta sanitaria) at your local health center (centro de salud). (5) Choose a primary care doctor (médico de cabecera) at the health center. WHAT'S COVERED: GP visits, specialist visits, hospital treatment, emergency care, surgeries, maternity care, vaccinations, prescription medications (40% copay for working adults, free for pensioners). WHAT'S NOT COVERED: Dental (except emergencies), vision (except medical conditions), cosmetic procedures. QUALITY: Spanish public hospitals are modern and well-equipped. Wait times: 1-7 days for GP appointment, 1-4 weeks for specialist, 2-6 months for non-urgent surgery. Emergency: walk-in, 2-6 hour wait. NON-EU VISITORS: Not covered by public healthcare. Buy travel insurance (€5-15/day) or annual expat insurance (€40-80/month). See our English-Speaking Doctors record for private options. TIPS: (1) EHIC covers emergency and necessary care only — not routine. (2) Bring your EHIC card AND a copy. (3) Public health centers (centros de salud) are the first point of contact — don't go directly to a hospital unless it's an emergency. (4) Emergency treatment cannot be refused regardless of insurance status.

sections.heading: Emergencies are free for everyone
sections.body: Spanish hospitals cannot refuse emergency treatment regardless of insurance, residency, or nationality. If it's a genuine emergency, go to any hospital ER — they will treat you first and ask questions later.

title: English-Speaking Doctors
summary: Private clinics: Clinica Britannia (€80-120), Sanitas, Ruber Internacional. Bring insurance card. Emergency: 112.
content: English-speaking doctors in Madrid. PRIVATE CLINICS (best for non-Spanish speakers): (1) Clinica Britannia (Calle Velázquez 28, Salamanca) — English-speaking GPs and specialists, popular with expats. €80-120/visit. Metro Velázquez L4. (2) Sanitas (multiple locations) — Bupa-owned, English-speaking, insurance plans from €50/month. (3) Hospital Ruber Internacional (Calle La Masó 38, Mirasierra) — top private hospital, English-speaking, 24/7 ER. (4) Clinica Universidad de Navarra (Calle Marquesado de Sta. Marta 1) — top research hospital, English-speaking. (5) IMC Hospitales (multiple) — English-speaking, international insurance accepted. (6) Doctor Madrid (doctormadrid.es) — English-speaking house call service, €120-180/visit. WHAT TO BRING: Passport/ID, insurance card (if you have one), list of medications, medical history. If self-paying, expect €80-150 for a GP visit, €100-200 for a specialist. INSURANCE: International plans (Cigna, Bupa, Allianz, AXA) are widely accepted. Spanish insurance (Sanitas, Adeslas, Asisa) from €40-80/month. EMERGENCY: 112 (free, multilingual, 24/7). For minor issues, try a pharmacy first (free advice) — see our Pharmacies record. TIPS: (1) Clinica Britannia is the most popular for English speakers — book ahead. (2) Most private clinics offer same-day appointments. (3) For prescriptions, the doctor will give you a paper prescription (receta) — take it to any pharmacy. (4) Spanish pharmacies can also recommend doctors. (5) EU citizens: use your EHIC for free public healthcare — see our Public Healthcare record.

title: Teach English in Spain
summary: Most common expat job. TEFL/CELTA required (€1,200-1,800, 4 weeks). Pay €12-18/hour. Auxiliares program: €1,000/mo, 16 hrs/week.
content: Teaching English in Madrid is the most common job for English-speaking expats. TWO MAIN PATHS: (1) Private language academies (academias) — hire year-round, no teaching degree required. Pay: €12-18/hour for private classes, €1,000-1,600/month full-time. You need a TEFL or CELTA certificate (120 hours, includes teaching practice). Top academies: Wall Street English, Berlitz, Kings College, Number 16. (2) Bilingual schools (concertados and privados) — more stable, better pay, but require a teaching license from your home country. Pay: €1,500-2,500/month, school year contract (September-June). Apply via the British Council or Spanish Ministry of Education's bilingual program. (3) The Spanish government's 'Auxiliares de Conversación' program brings 1,500+ native English speakers per year to assist in Spanish schools — €1,000/month stipend, 16 hours/week, no teaching experience required. Apply October-February for the following September at professores.gob.es. REQUIREMENTS: TEFL or CELTA (120 hours with practicum) for academias. Bachelor's degree (any subject) for the Auxiliares program. Native or near-native English. EU citizenship OR a work visa (the Auxiliares program provides a student visa). FINDING JOBS: (1) InfoJobs (infojobs.net) — search 'profesor de inglés'. (2) LinkedIn. (3) Direct applications to academies (walk in with your CV). (4) Auxiliares program (professores.gob.es). (5) Facebook groups: 'English Teachers Madrid', 'TEFL Madrid Jobs'. TIPS: (1) Get your TEFL/CELTA BEFORE arriving — it's much harder to find work without it. (2) September-October is peak hiring season. (3) Most academy work is part-time (20-25 hours/week) — combine 2-3 academies for full-time income. (4) Private students (€20-30/hour) supplement academy income — advertise on tusclasesparticulares.es and Superprof. (5) The Auxiliares program is the easiest entry point — no TEFL required, but only €1,000/month (need to supplement with private classes).

title: Museo del Prado
summary: Spain's #1 museum. Velázquez, Goya, El Greco, Bosch, Rubens, Titian. €15, free Mon-Sat 6-8pm and Sundays 5-7pm.
content: Museo del Prado is Spain's most important art museum and one of the world's greatest. Located at Paseo del Prado (Metro Banco de España L2 or Atocha L1). The collection: 8,000+ paintings, 700+ sculptures, 5,000+ drawings, 2,000+ prints. SPANISH MASTERS: (1) Velázquez — 'Las Meninas' (Room 12), 'The Surrender of Breda', 'The Spinners'. The Prado has 50+ Velázquez works — the world's largest collection. (2) Goya — 'The Third of May 1808' (Room 64), 'The Naked Maja' and 'The Clothed Maja', the Black Paintings. 150+ Goya works. (3) El Greco — 'The Nobleman with his Hand on his Chest', 'The Trinity'. OTHER HIGHLIGHTS: (4) Bosch — 'The Garden of Earthly Delights' (Room 56A) — surreal 16th-century triptych. (5) Rubens — 100+ works. (6) Titian, Tintoretto, Veronese — Venetian masters. (7) Dürer, Cranach — Northern Renaissance. ADMISSION: €15 general, €7.50 reduced. Free: Mon-Sat 6-8pm, Sun 5-7pm. Free always for under 18, over 65, unemployed, teachers. HOURS: Mon-Sat 10am-8pm, Sun 10am-7pm. Closed Jan 1, May 1, Dec 25. TIPS: (1) Don't try to see everything — focus on the Spanish masters (Velázquez, Goya, El Greco). (2) Allow 3-4 hours minimum. (3) The free evening hours are crowded but manageable. (4) Skip-the-line tickets online (€15) are worth it on busy days. (5) Audio guide €5 (English) — or use the free Prado app. (6) The most famous paintings (Las Meninas, Third of May, Garden of Earthly Delights) have crowd control — expect 5-10 min waits. (7) Photography allowed (no flash) except in the Guernica room at Reina Sofía. (8) The Prado's café is overpriced — eat nearby at Taberna El Sur or La Campana.

sections.heading: Free Entry Hack
sections.body: Monday–Saturday 6–8pm and Sunday 5–7pm = FREE. But you still need to book online first — walk-ups are not accepted during the free slot. Reserve your free ticket on the official Prado website up to 1 hour before the free window starts.

items.title: Las Meninas — Diego Velázquez (1656)
items.body: The most enigmatic painting in art history — a mirror within a mirror. Room 12.
meta: Best time: Early morning or late afternoon

items.title: The Third of May 1808 — Francisco Goya (1814)
items.body: Goya's powerful depiction of war's brutality. Room 64.

items.title: The Garden of Earthly Delights — Hieronymus Bosch (c. 1515)
items.body: A surreal triptych that's like Medieval Netflix. Room 56A.

items.title: Best Times to Visit
items.body: Tuesday–Thursday 10am or after 4pm. Avoid weekend mornings like the plague.

items.title: Skip the Audio Guide
items.body: Download the free Prado app instead. It's updated more frequently and has more content than the paid €5 audio guide.

items.title: Wear Comfy Shoes
items.body: You'll walk 2–3km inside! Your feet will thank you later.

items.title: Photography Rules
items.body: Photos allowed (no flash)! But crowds gather around famous works.

title: Museo Nacional Centro de Arte Reina Sofía
summary: Spain's national modern art museum. Picasso's Guernica, Dalí, Miró. €12, free Mon/Wed-Sat 7-9pm. Closed Tuesdays.
content: Museo Nacional Centro de Arte Reina Sofía is Spain's national museum of 20th-century art. Located at Calle de Santa Isabel 52 (Metro Atocha L1). The museum's masterpiece is Picasso's 'Guernica' (1937) — one of the most famous paintings in the world, depicting the bombing of Guernica during the Spanish Civil War. THE COLLECTION: (1) Picasso — Guernica (Room 206, 2nd floor), plus studies and earlier works. (2) Dalí — Surrealist works including 'The Great Masturbator' and 'Soft Construction with Boiled Beans'. (3) Miró — Abstract and surrealist paintings. (4) Tàpies — Catalan abstract. (5) Other Spanish artists: Gris, Gargallo, Julio González. (6) International: Calder, Bourgeois, Beuys. BUILDING: The main building (Sabatini Building) was a 16th-century hospital, renovated by Jean Nouvel in 2005. The Nouvel extension has a striking red roof and modern exhibition spaces. ADMISSION: €12 general, free on Mon/Wed-Sat 7-9pm and Sun 1:30-2:30pm. Free for under 18, over 65, students. HOURS: Mon, Wed-Sat 10am-9pm (extension until 11pm Fri), Sun 10am-2:30pm. Closed Tuesdays, Jan 1, May 1, Dec 25. TIPS: (1) Guernica is in Room 206 — go there first, then explore. No photos allowed in the Guernica room. (2) Allow 2-3 hours. (3) The free evening hours (7-9pm) are busy but manageable. (4) The museum is huge — pick 2-3 wings and focus. (5) Audio guide €4 (English). (6) The café on the 2nd floor is good and reasonably priced. (7) Combine with the Prado (10 min walk) and Thyssen (10 min walk) for the 'Golden Triangle of Art'.

title: Royal Palace (Palacio Real)
summary: Europe's largest royal palace by floor area. 3,418 rooms, 135,000m². €12 entry, free for EU Mon-Thu 4-6pm (off-season). 2 hours.
content: The Royal Palace of Madrid (Palacio Real) is the official residence of the Spanish Royal Family, though they don't actually live here (they live in the smaller Zarzuela Palace on the outskirts). It's the largest royal palace in Europe by floor area — 135,000m², 3,418 rooms, 870 windows, 240 balconies, 44 staircases. HISTORY: Built 1738-1764 on the site of the old Alcázar (which burned down in 1734). Designed by Italian architects (Sachetti, Juvarra). King Carlos III was the first monarch to live here (1764). Last monarch to live here full-time: Alfonso XIII (until 1931). Now used for state ceremonies and tourism. WHAT TO SEE: (1) Throne Room (Salón del Trono) — gold and crimson, with the thrones of King Felipe VI and Queen Letizia. (2) Royal Armory (Real Armería) — one of the world's best collections of armor and weapons. (3) Royal Kitchen (Cocina Real) — recently opened, 18th-century kitchens. (4) Painting Gallery — works by Velázquez, Goya, Caravaggio, Tiepolo. (5) Stradivarius Room — 2 Stradivarius violins and 2 cellos. (6) The Main Staircase — 72 steps, single piece of marble. (7) Royal Chapel — with an organ. (8) The Crown Room — the Spanish crown and scepter. ADMISSION: €12 general, €6 reduced. Free for EU citizens Mon-Thu 4-6pm (Oct-Mar only) — bring passport. Free for under 5, disabled. HOURS: Mon-Sat 10am-6pm (Oct-Mar), 10am-8pm (Apr-Sep). Sun 10am-4pm. Closed: Jan 1, Jan 6, May 1, Dec 25. TIPS: (1) Allow 2 hours minimum. (2) Book skip-the-line tickets online (€13) — lines can be 1+ hours. (3) Photography allowed (no flash) except in the Throne Room. (4) Free EU entry (Mon-Thu 4-6pm, Oct-Mar) is great but crowded. (5) The Royal Armory is included in the ticket — don't skip it. (6) Combine with Almudena Cathedral (2 min), Sabatini Gardens (5 min), Plaza de Oriente (5 min). (7) The changing of the guard happens on the first Wednesday of each month (11am, free).

items.title: Throne Room (Salón del Trono)
items.body: Gold and crimson, with the thrones of King Felipe VI and Queen Letizia.

items.title: Royal Armory (Real Armería)
items.body: One of the world's best collections of armor and weapons, including Charles V's suit.

items.title: Main Staircase
items.body: 72 steps carved from a single piece of San Agustín marble. A baroque masterpiece.

items.title: Royal Chapel
items.body: With a 1778 pipe organ still used for concerts.

items.title: Crown Room
items.body: The Spanish crown and scepter — relatively simple compared to other European monarchies.

sections.heading: Changing of the Guard
sections.body: First Wednesday of each month at 11am (free). The Solemn Changing (cambio solemne) is the full ceremony with guards on horseback — worth planning your visit around.

title: Catedral de la Almudena
summary: Madrid's main cathedral, next to Royal Palace. Built 1879-1993. Free crypt, €6 museum + dome. Beautiful modern ceiling paintings.
content: Catedral de Santa María la Real de la Almudena is Madrid's main cathedral, located right next to the Royal Palace. Construction started in 1879 but wasn't completed until 1993 (interrupted by the Civil War and funding crises). Pope John Paul II consecrated it in 1993. ARCHITECTURE: A mix of neoclassical (exterior), neo-Gothic (interior), and contemporary (the brightly colored ceiling paintings by Kiko Argüello, completed 2003). WHAT TO SEE: (1) The main nave — neo-Gothic with modern ceiling paintings (colorful and somewhat controversial). (2) The crypt (entrance on Calle Mayor) — FREE to visit, 400+ columns, 16 chapels, atmospheric. (3) The museum (€6) — treasury, liturgical vestments, access to the dome with great views over the Royal Palace. (4) The choir and high altar. HOURS: Cathedral open daily 10am-2:30pm and 4:30-7:30pm. Crypt open daily 10am-8pm. Museum open daily 10am-2:30pm. COST: Cathedral entry FREE. Crypt FREE. Museum €6. MASS: Mon-Sat 8pm, Sun 10:30am, 12pm, 1:30pm, 6pm, 8pm. Sundays: free organ concert at 1:30pm (Sept-June). TIPS: (1) The crypt is the best free thing to see — atmospheric, beautiful, often empty. (2) The museum's dome has the best view of the Royal Palace. (3) The cathedral is most beautiful at sunset — view from Plaza de Oriente. (4) Dress code: no bare shoulders or short shorts (it's a cathedral). (5) The modern ceiling paintings are controversial — some love them, some hate them. Judge for yourself. (6) Combine with the Royal Palace (2 min walk) and Sabatini Gardens (5 min walk).

title: Parque del Retiro
summary: Madrid's #1 park, 118 hectares. Rowing lake, Crystal Palace, Rose Garden, 15,000 trees. Free. Open 6am-midnight.
content: Parque del Retiro (or simply 'El Retiro') is Madrid's most famous park — 118 hectares of gardens, monuments, and recreational areas in the center of the city. Once a royal park (reserved for the Spanish monarchy until 1868), now free and open to all. TOP THINGS TO DO: (1) Rowing lake (Estanque Grande) — rowboats €6/45 min. The iconic Alfonso XII monument overlooks the lake. (2) Crystal Palace (Palacio de Cristal) — 1887 glass-and-iron pavilion, now hosts temporary art exhibitions (free). (3) Rose Garden (Rosaleda) — 4,000+ roses, best May-June. (4) Paseo de la Argentina (Statue Walk) — statues of Spanish kings lining a tree-lined avenue. (5) Jardín de Vivaces (perennial garden) — colorful year-round. (6) Parterre (formal French garden) — geometric hedges and fountains. (7) Fountain of the Falling Angel (Fuente del Ángel Caído) — the world's only public statue of Lucifer. (8) Children's playgrounds (3 different areas). (9) BiciMAD station — rent a bike. (10) Open-air exercise equipment (free). PRACTICAL: Open 6am-midnight year-round. Free. Metro: Retiro (L2), Banco de España (L2), Atocha (L1), Ibiza (L9). TIPS: (1) Best time: early morning (peaceful, joggers) or late afternoon (golden light, families). (2) Allow 2-3 hours to explore. (3) The park is huge — get a map at any entrance. (4) Free Sunday concerts at the bandstand (April-October). (5) The Crystal Palace sometimes has long lines — go early. (6) Watch for pickpockets near the lake and main entrances. (7) Combine with the Prado (5 min walk north) or Reina Sofía (10 min walk southwest).

items.title: Rowing Lake (Estanque Grande)
items.body: Rowboats €6/45 min. The iconic Alfonso XII monument overlooks the lake.

items.title: Crystal Palace (Palacio de Cristal)
items.body: 1887 glass-and-iron pavilion, hosts free contemporary art exhibitions.

items.title: Rose Garden (Rosaleda)
items.body: 4,000+ roses. Best in May–June.

items.title: Statue Walk (Paseo de la Argentina)
items.body: Statues of Spanish kings lining a tree-lined avenue.

items.title: Fountain of the Fallen Angel
items.body: The world's only public statue of Lucifer. Pics or it didn't happen.

sections.heading: Best time to visit
sections.body: Early morning (7–9am) for peace and joggers, or late afternoon (5–7pm) for golden light and families. Avoid Sunday afternoons (packed). Free Sunday concerts at the bandstand April–October.

title: Casa de Campo
summary: Madrid's largest park, 1,535 hectares. Zoo, amusement park, cable car, lake, trails. 5x Central Park. Metro Lago/Batán.
content: Casa de Campo is Madrid's largest green space — 1,535 hectares (5x Central Park), west of the Royal Palace across the Manzanares River. Once a royal hunting ground, now a public park. WHAT'S INSIDE: (1) Lake (Lago de la Casa de Campo) — rowboats €6/hour, restaurants around the lake (Mi Suegra, El Lago). (2) Zoo Aquarium de Madrid — see our separate record. (3) Parque de Atracciones — Madrid's main amusement park. See our separate record. (4) Teleférico (cable car) — currently CLOSED for renovation until Q1 2027. (5) MTB and trail running — 30+ km of trails, hilly, wooded. (6) Picnic areas (free, bring your own food). (7) Madrid Amusement Park. GETTING THERE: Metro Lago (L10), Batán (L10), Casa de Campo (L5). TIPS: (1) The park is huge — plan which area you want to visit. (2) Best for: families, runners, cyclists, half-day escapes. (3) Avoid alone at night — the park is known for nighttime sex work along the main road. Daytime is safe and family-friendly. (4) Bring water — few fountains. (5) The lake area has the best facilities (restaurants, boat rental). (6) You can walk from the Royal Palace (15 min) via the Sabatini Gardens and down through Parque del Oeste.

title: Madrid Río
summary: 10km linear park along the Manzanares River. Playgrounds, bike paths, fountains, urban beach. Free. Metro Príncipe Pío.
content: Madrid Río is a 10km linear park along the Manzanares River, created in 2011 when the M-30 highway was put underground. Runs from Príncipe Pío (north) to Getafe (south). HIGHLIGHTS: (1) 17 play areas for kids — the most famous is the 'Hammock' swings and the giant slide. The playgrounds are among the best in Europe. (2) The Fountain Walk (Salón de Pinos) — 30 fountains, great for cooling off in summer. Free. (3) Arganzuela Beach (Playa de Arganzuela) — urban beach with 3 pools, open June-August, FREE. (4) Puente de Toledo — 18th-century baroque bridge, Instagram-worthy. (5) BiciMAD stations throughout — rent a bike and ride the full 10km. (6) Jardines de Puerta de Toledo and Jardines del Puente de Segovia — formal gardens. (7) Matadero Madrid (contemporary arts center) at the south end. GETTING THERE: Metro Príncipe Pío (L6, L10), Puerta de Toledo (L5), Marqués de Vadillo (L5). COST: Free, open 24/7. TIPS: (1) Best for families with kids — the playgrounds are amazing. (2) Best for runners and cyclists — flat, paved, 10km. (3) Best for summer cooling — the fountains and urban beach. (4) Combine with Matadero Madrid (contemporary art) for a full day. (5) The park connects to Casa de Campo (west) and Retiro (east, via Paseo de la Rey). (6) At night, the park is well-lit and safe.

title: Teleférico (Cable Car)
summary: Cable car from the Rosaleda in Retiro Park to Casa de Campo. 11 min, panoramic views.
content:
€6.50 one-way, €9.20 round-trip. Operates 11 AM to dusk (closed in high winds). Not the most dramatic cable car in the world but a pleasant ride and a fun way to connect the two parks. Combine with Retiro and a picnic in Casa de Campo.

⚠️ UPDATE 2026: The Madrid Teleférico is TEMPORARILY CLOSED for renovation. A €26.6 million investment will bring 47 new cabins. Expected reopening: Q1 2027. Check the official website for updates. In the meantime, alternative viewpoints: Temple of Debod (free), Círculo de Bellas Artes rooftop (€5), Riu Plaza España rooftop (€10).

title: Plaza Mayor
summary: 17th-century arcaded square. Once the heart of Madrid — markets, bullfights, executions. Now tourist hub. €3.50 bocata de calamares.
content: Plaza Mayor is Madrid's most iconic square — a grand arcaded plaza built 1617-1619 (during the reign of Philip III, whose statue stands in the center). Designed by architect Juan Gómez de Mora. 129m × 94m, enclosed by 3-story residential buildings with 237 balconies. HISTORY: Once the heart of Madrid's civic life — markets, bullfights, executions, coronations, and Inquisition trials all happened here. The Inquisition's 'autos de fe' (public trials) drew crowds of 30,000+. WHAT TO DO: (1) Walk the arcades — 9 entrance archways, each different. (2) Eat a bocata de calamares (fried squid sandwich, €3.50) at Bar La Campana (Calle de la Botoneras 6, southwest corner) — a Madrid tradition. (3) Visit the Tourist Office (ground floor, Plaza Mayor 3) — free maps, English advice. (4) Shop for souvenirs — stamp shops, coin dealers, and the traditional 'mesón' restaurants (touristy but atmospheric). (5) Watch street performers and listen to buskers. (6) Photography — the square is stunning at golden hour. EVENTS: Christmas market (December), San Isidro festival (May), concerts and cultural events year-round. COST: Free to enter and walk around. TIPS: (1) Restaurants ON the Plaza Mayor are overpriced and mediocre — eat your bocata de calamares standing at the bar (€3.50) but don't sit down for a full meal. (2) The best views are from the arcades on the upper floors. (3) Watch for pickpockets — this is Madrid's #1 pickpocket spot. (4) The Plaza Mayor is 2 min walk from Puerta del Sol. (5) At night, the square is beautifully lit and much quieter.

sections.heading: Bocata de Calamares
sections.body: The Madrid tradition: eat a fried squid sandwich (€3.50) at Bar La Campana (SW corner). Don't sit down for a full meal on the plaza — restaurants are overpriced and mediocre. Walk 5 min to Cava Baja for real tapas.

items.title: Walk the arcades
items.body: 9 entrance archways, each different. The upper-floor arcades have the best views.

items.title: Tourist Office
items.body: Ground floor (Plaza Mayor 3). Free maps, English advice. Open 9:30am–8:30pm.

items.title: Stamp and coin shops
items.body: Under the arcades — a relic of old Madrid. Interesting even if you don't collect.

items.title: Christmas market
items.body: December — stalls selling nativity figures, gifts, and hot chestnuts. Magical at night.

title: Gran Vía
summary: Madrid's Broadway — theaters, cinemas, shopping, iconic architecture. 1.3km from Alcalá to Plaza de España. Free to walk.
content: Gran Vía ('Great Road') is Madrid's main commercial and entertainment boulevard — 1.3km from Calle de Alcalá (east) to Plaza de España (west). Often called the 'Spanish Broadway' for its concentration of theaters and cinemas. ARCHITECTURE: Built 1910-1929, Gran Vía showcases 3 architectural phases: (1) Edificio Metrópolis (1911, French Haussmann style) at the east end — iconic winged statue. (2) Mid-section: Chicago School skyscrapers (Telefónica Building, 1929 — Europe's first skyscraper). (3) West end: Art Deco and rationalist buildings (Capitol building, 1933). WHAT TO DO: (1) Shopping — Zara flagship, Primark, H&M, Mango, Apple Store. Open Mon-Sat 10am-10pm. (2) Theaters — musicals, plays, concerts (Lope de Vega, Coliseum, Compac Gran Vía). (3) Rooftops — Riu Plaza España (€10, highest in Madrid), Hotel Urban. (4) Architecture walking tour — free, self-guided. (5) Cinema — Cinesa Gran Vía (mainstream), Cine Doré (art house, €3). METRO: Gran Vía (Lines 1, 5), Plaza de España (Lines 3, 10), Banco de España (Line 2). TIPS: (1) Walk it east to west (downhill) — start at Metrópolis, end at Plaza de España. (2) Best time: evening when the buildings are lit up. (3) Avoid eating ON Gran Vía (tourist traps) — walk 2 blocks north to Malasaña or south to Huertas for better food. (4) The Gran Vía is constantly under renovation — check for scaffolding. (5) The Apple Store (Gran Vía 54) is worth visiting for the architecture alone. (6) For the best photo: from the rooftop of the Círculo de Bellas Artes (€5) looking down Gran Vía.

sections.heading: What to Do on Gran Vía

items.title: Shopping
items.body: Zara flagship, Primark, H&M, Mango, Apple Store. Open Mon–Sat 10am–10pm.

items.title: Theaters
items.body: Musicals, plays, concerts at Lope de Vega, Coliseum, Compac Gran Vía. Spain's 'Broadway'.

items.title: Rooftop bars
items.body: Riu Plaza España (€10, highest in Madrid), Hotel Urban (cocktails). Best at sunset.

items.title: Architecture walk
items.body: Edificio Metrópolis (1911), Telefónica (1929), Capitol (1933). 3 architectural eras in 1km.

sections.heading: Dining tip
sections.body: Avoid eating ON Gran Vía — tourist traps. Walk 2 blocks north to Malasaña (indie cafés, craft beer) or south to Huertas (traditional tapas) for better food at half the price.

title: Puerta del Sol
summary: Madrid's kilometer zero — the center of Spain. Clock tower, bear statue, New Year's Eve gathering. Free. Always busy.
content: Puerta del Sol ('Gate of the Sun') is Madrid's central square and the symbolic heart of Spain. WHAT TO SEE: (1) Kilometer Zero plaque (on the ground near the Casa de Correos) — the official center of Spain's radial road network. All distances are measured from here. (2) Casa de Correos (1768) — the old post office, now the seat of the Madrid regional government. The clock tower on top is where Spaniards gather on New Year's Eve to eat 12 grapes at midnight (broadcast on national TV). (3) El Oso y el Madroño (The Bear and the Strawberry Tree) — a 20-tonne statue of a bear eating from a madroño tree, the symbol of Madrid. Everyone takes a photo here.
