(5) Federico's Churrería (Calle de la Duque de Alba 4, La Latina) — locals' favorite for breakfast, €3. WHAT TO ORDER: 'Churros con chocolate' (6 churros + chocolate, €4-4.50). 'Porras con chocolate' (3 porras + chocolate, €4-4.50). 'Mixto' (3 churros + 2 porras, €4.50). 'Chocolate a la taza' (just chocolate, €3). The chocolate is thick, dark, and bittersweet — not like hot chocolate, more like a dipping sauce. Eat with your hands. Dip, bite, repeat. TIPS: (1) Go at 4am on a Saturday — it's a Madrid tradition after a night out. (2) The chocolate is very thick — if you want it thinner, ask for 'chocolate más claro'. (3) Coffee is also served with churros for breakfast — 'café con churros' (€3-4). (4) The line at San Ginés looks long but moves fast (5-10 min). (5) For a less touristy experience, go to Chocolatería 1902 or any neighborhood churrería.

sections.heading: Best Chocolaterías
rows: Older than San Ginés, less touristy

title: Bingo Halls in Madrid
summary: Bingo is popular with older Spaniards. Bingo Canoa (Alcalá 33), Bingo Gran Vía. €5-10 for a session. Tobacco + drinks.
content: Bingo halls are a Madrid institution — mostly older Spaniards, smoky, social, and surprisingly fun. MAIN HALLS: (1) Bingo Canoa (Calle de Alcalá 33, Metro Sevilla L2) — largest, most central. Sessions every 2 hours from 12pm. €5-10 per session (includes bingo cards + a drink). Open daily 11am-3am. (2) Bingo Gran Vía (Calle Gran Vía 39, Metro Gran Vía L1/L5) — central, touristy. €6-12. (3) Bingo Alcalá (Calle de Alcalá 350, Metro Suanzes L5) — local, east Madrid. €4-8. (4) Bingo Leganés (south Madrid) — for locals. WHAT TO EXPECT: (1) Sessions last 60-90 minutes, with 8-12 games per session. (2) Cards (cartones) cost €1-3 each — buy 2-4 for a session. (3) Electronic bingo machines available (more expensive but more cards). (4) Drinks and tapas served at your table (included in the session price). (5) The atmosphere is smoky (despite smoking bans, some bingo halls still allow it or have smoking areas). (6) Prizes range from €20 (line) to €500+ (full house). TIPS: (1) Bingo is in Spanish — learn the numbers 1-90 or use an electronic machine. (2) The atmosphere is older and local — not a tourist activity. (3) Go with Spanish-speaking friends for the full experience. (4) Free drink + tapa included — the €5-10 session is good value even if you don't win. (5) Most halls are open from 11am to 3am.

title: Círculo de Bellas Artes Rooftop
summary: Best 360° views of Madrid from this cultural center rooftop. €5 entry (no drink required). Café, bar, sunset spot. Calle Alcalá 42.
content: The Círculo de Bellas Artes (Calle de Alcalá 42, Metro Banco de España L2) is a cultural center with the best rooftop terrace in Madrid for value. Unlike hotel rooftops (Riu €10, Hotel Urban €15), the Círculo charges just €5 to go up — and you don't have to buy a drink. THE ROOFTOP: 360° views over Gran Vía, Banco de España, Plaza de Cibeles, and the Retiro. The rooftop has: (1) An open-air bar (drinks €6-10, coffee €2). (2) Lounge chairs and tables. (3) A small rooftop café serving snacks. (4) An iconic statue of Minerva (the rooftop's symbol). (5) The highest publicly accessible viewpoint in central Madrid (7th floor + rooftop). ENTRY: €5 general (includes access to rooftop + current art exhibitions). €4 reduced. Open Tue-Sun 10am-9pm (until 2pm in August). TIPS: (1) Best time: 1 hour before sunset for golden light photos. (2) The rooftop is the best value view in Madrid — €5 vs €10-15 at hotel rooftops. (3) No dress code (unlike hotel rooftops). (4) The building itself (1926, architect Antonio Palacios) is beautiful — a neo-baroque masterpiece. (5) Combine with a walk down Gran Vía or a visit to the Bank of Spain (across the street). (6) The rooftop hosts occasional concerts and events — check cbmadrid.es. (7) For the absolute highest view in Madrid: the Riu Plaza España rooftop (€10, 27th floor) is higher but more expensive and crowded.

title: Spas & Wellness Centers in Madrid
summary: Day spas: Four Seasons Spa, Riu Plaza España spa, public spas at municipal sports centers. €30-120. Massage, thermal circuits.
content: Spas and wellness centers in Madrid. LUXURY HOTEL SPAS: (1) Four Seasons Spa (Calle de Sevilla 3) — Madrid's most luxurious spa. 4 treatment rooms, thermal circuit (pool, sauna, steam room, ice fountain). Day pass €120 (includes robe, slippers, water). Massages €150-250. (2) Riu Plaza España Hotel Spa (Gran Vía 137) — 21st floor spa with pool and city views. Day pass €50. (3) Hotel Urban Spa (Carrera de San Jerónimo 5) — small but chic. Day pass €40. PUBLIC/ AFFORDABLE: (1) Centro Deportivo Municipal La Elipa — municipal sports center with spa (pool, sauna, steam room). €8/visit. Very basic but clean. (2) Centro Deportivo Municipal San Blas — similar, €8/visit. (3) Piscinas de Vicálvaro — has a small spa area, €6. INDEPENDENT SPAS: (1) Tantra Spa Madrid (Calle de la Orense 31) — massages and treatments, €40-80. (2) Bangkok Thai Spa (Calle de la Príncipe de Vergara 50) — Thai massage, €50-90. (3) Watermassage (multiple) — water massage beds, €15-25. HAMMAM: See our Hammam Al Ándalus record (€40-110, Arab baths with thermal circuit). TIPS: (1) The Four Seasons is the best splurge — book 1 week ahead. (2) Municipal sports center spas are basic but €8 is unbeatable. (3) For just a massage (no spa access), Thai and tantric spas are €40-80. (4) Most hotel spas require advance booking — call ahead. (5) Bring a swimsuit for any spa with a pool or thermal circuit.

title: El Corte Inglés Castellana (Flagship)
summary: Spain's iconic department store. 7 floors: fashion, food, electronics, books, toys, gourmet. Price-matches competitors. Open Sundays.
content: El Corte Inglés Castellana (Paseo de la Castellana 53, Metro Nuevos Ministerios L6/L8/L10) is Spain's largest department store and a Madrid institution. 7 FLOORS: (1) Basement — supermarket (Hipercor), gourmet food hall, wine cellar. (2) Ground floor — cosmetics, perfumes, accessories. (3) 1st floor — women's fashion (Spanish and international brands). (4) 2nd floor — men's fashion, sports. (5) 3rd floor — home, electronics, toys. (6) 4th floor — books, music, stationery (best English book section in Madrid). (7) 5th floor — restaurants, cafeteria. SERVICES: (1) Price matching — show a lower price from MediaMarkt, FNAC, or Amazon.es and they'll match it. (2) Tax-free shopping — non-EU residents can claim 15-21% VAT back (see our VAT Refund record). (3) Gift wrapping (free). (4) English-speaking customer service. (5) Foreign currency exchange (fair rates). (6) Restaurant and cafeteria (6th floor, good value lunch menú €12-15). HOURS: Mon-Sat 10am-10pm, Sun 11am-9pm (one of the few stores open Sundays). TIPS: (1) The gourmet food hall (basement) is excellent for Spanish products: jamón, cheese, wine, olive oil. (2) The book section (4th floor) has the best English book selection in Madrid. (3) Price matching is real — show them a screenshot of the lower price on your phone. (4) The cafeteria (5th floor) has great views of Castellana and a €12 lunch menú. (5) Other El Corte Inglés locations: Goya, Preciados, Princesa — Castellana is the largest. (6) Look for 'rebajas' (sale) signs in January and July.

title: Loewe Flagship Store (Spanish Luxury)
summary: Spain's most famous luxury brand, founded 1846. Flagship at Calle Serrano 26. Leather goods, fashion, €200-5,000+. Tax-free available.
content: Loewe (Calle de Serrano 26, Metro Serrano L4) is Spain's most prestigious luxury brand — founded in Madrid in 1846 as a leather cooperative. The flagship store on Serrano is a must-visit for luxury shoppers. THE STORE: 3 floors in a beautifully restored 19th-century building. Ground floor: leather bags (Amazona, Puzzle, Flamenco — €500-2,500), accessories, small leather goods. 1st floor: women's ready-to-wear (€800-5,000+). 2nd floor: men's collection and the Loewe Foundation art space (free exhibitions). LOEWO HISTORY: Founded 1846, the brand has been the official supplier to the Spanish Royal Family since 1905. Creative director Jonathan Anderson (since 2013) has transformed Loewe into one of fashion's most innovative houses. The Puzzle bag (designed by Anderson) is the iconic modern piece. THE AMAZONA (1975) is the classic. PRICES: Small leather goods (cardholders, wallets) €200-400. Bags €500-2,500. Ready-to-wear €800-5,000+. Scarves and accessories €150-400. TAX-FREE: Non-EU residents can claim 15-21% VAT back on purchases over €90. Ask for the 'Tax Free form' at checkout — see our VAT Refund record. TIPS: (1) The store itself is beautiful — worth visiting even if you're just browsing. (2) The Loewe Foundation art space (2nd floor) is free and shows rotating contemporary art. (3) Staff are multilingual and not pushy. (4) The gift wrapping is legendary — exquisite boxes and ribbons. (5) For vintage Loewe, check the resale market (Vestiaire Collective, The RealReal). (6) Other Loewe locations: Gran Vía 28, El Corte Inglés Castellana.

title: El Rastro Antiques & Collectibles
summary: The antiques section of El Rastro flea market. Calle Ribera de Curtidores, Sundays 9am-3pm. Vintage posters, furniture, religious art.
content: The antiques section of El Rastro flea market is on Calle Ribera de Curtidores (La Latina, Metro La Latina L5) — the main street of the market, closest to Plaza de Cascorro. This is where serious collectors go. WHAT YOU'LL FIND: (1) Vintage Spanish posters (travel posters from the 1930s-1960s, film posters, political posters) — €20-200. (2) Antique fans (abanicos) — hand-painted, Spanish-made, €15-80. (3) Religious art (retablos, santos, crucifixes) — €30-300. (4) Old photographs of Madrid — €10-50. (5) Antique furniture (chairs, tables, mirrors) — €50-500. (6) Vintage watches and jewelry — €20-500. (7) Books and maps — €5-100. (8) Military memorabilia (medals, uniforms) — €20-200. (9) Vinyl records — €5-30. (10) Toys and tin soldiers — €10-100. TIPS: (1) Go early (9-10am) for the best finds — serious collectors arrive at 9am sharp. (2) Bargaining is expected on items over €20 — offer 60-70% of the asking price and negotiate. (3) Bring cash (most stalls don't take cards). (4) Check for damage before buying — 'antique' can mean 'broken'. (5) The best stalls are permanent shops on Ribera de Curtidores (open Sundays only for the market, but their inventory is curated). (6) For authentic vintage posters, look for the specialist stalls near Calle de la Mira. (7) Watch your wallet — El Rastro is Madrid's #1 pickpocket spot.

title: Movistar Estudiantes Basketball
summary: Madrid's historic basketball club (founded 1948). Currently in LEB Oro (2nd division). Smaller, intimate atmosphere. €10-25 tickets.
content: Movistar Estudiantes is Madrid's second basketball club (after Real Madrid Baloncesto). Founded in 1948 by students at the Instituto Ramiro de Maeztu (hence 'Estudiantes' = Students). Currently in LEB Oro (second division) after financial difficulties, but with a passionate fan base. VENUE: Polideportivo Antonio Magariños (Calle de Serrano 37, Metro Rubén Darío L5). Capacity 2,000 — one of the most intimate basketball venues in Spain. You're right on top of the action. TICKETS: €10-25. Buy at the door or at estudiantes.cl. Season: October-May. HOURS: Games typically Saturday 6pm or Sunday 12:30pm. TIPS: (1) The atmosphere at Magariños is legendary — small, loud, and passionate. Even in the second division, the fans are some of the most devoted in Spanish basketball. (2) Estudiantes has produced NBA players: Carlos Jiménez, Felipe Reyes (who later became a Real Madrid legend). (3) The venue is inside a school — the entrance is on Serrano, look for the 'Magariños' sign. (4) Tickets are cheap and available at the door — no need to book ahead. (5) For top-level basketball, see our Real Madrid Baloncesto record (EuroLeague, €20-60).

title: Parkrun & Free Group Runs in Madrid
summary: Free weekly 5K parkrun at Casa de Campo (Sat 9am). Plus Hash House Harriers, Madrid Running Club. Free, social, all levels.
content: Free organized running in Madrid. PARKRUN: Free 5K timed run every Saturday at 9am in Casa de Campo (start near the lake). Register free at parkrun.es. Barcode system — scan after the run for your time. 100-300 runners weekly. All levels welcome (walkers too). Post-run coffee at the lake café. FREE, social, well-organized. OTHER FREE GROUP RUNS: (1) Madrid Hash House Harriers — 'a drinking club with a running problem'. Runs every 2 weeks, various locations. €3 (includes beer). Facebook: 'Madrid HHH'. Social, fun, not competitive. (2) Madrid Running Club — meets Thursday 7pm at Puerta del Sol. 5-10km easy run through the center. Free. English-speaking. (3) Lululemon Community Run — Saturday 10am from Lululemon Gran Vía store. 5km, free, free swag. (4) Nike Run Club — Tuesday 7pm from Nike Gran Vía. Free, coach-led. (5) Madrid Trail Running — weekend trail runs in the Sierra de Guadarrama. Facebook group. RUNNING STORES WITH GROUP RUNS: (1) Running Company (Calle de la Hermanos García Noblejas 49) — Thursday 7pm. (2) Boston Runners (Calle de la Alcalá 110) — Wednesday 7pm. TIPS: (1) Parkrun is the best — free, timed, and the Casa de Campo route is beautiful. (2) Summer: run before 9am (too hot after). (3) Winter: mornings can be near freezing — dress in layers. (4) All groups welcome beginners — don't be intimidated. (5) For race registration, see our Running in Madrid record.

title: Printable Tourist Map PDF (esMadrid)
summary: Free printable PDF tourist map from esMadrid. Shows attractions, Metro, tourist offices. Download before you go.
content: The official esMadrid printable tourist map is the best free map for visitors. DOWNLOAD: esmadrid.com/en/madrid-city-map — PDF available in English, Spanish, French, German. WHAT IT SHOWS: (1) Central Madrid (inside the M-30 ring) in detail. (2) Major tourist attractions marked with icons. (3) Metro lines (color-coded). (4) Tourist offices (i symbol). (5) Police stations. (6) Hospitals. (7) Parks. (8) Main streets and plazas named. PRINTING TIPS: (1) Print at A3 size (11x17) for readability — A4 is too small for the detail. (2) Print in color — the Metro line colors are essential. (3) Print double-sided: central map on one side, greater Madrid inset on the other. (4) Laminate if you can — it'll survive a week in your pocket. ALTERNATIVES: (1) Pick up a free paper copy at any Tourist Office (Plaza Mayor, airport, Atocha). (2) Download the esMadrid app (English) for the same map with GPS. (3) Google Maps offline download (200MB, covers all of Madrid). TIPS: (1) The official map is more accurate than Google Maps for pedestrian zones and one-way streets in the old town. (2) The PDF works offline — no data needed. (3) Save it to your phone AND print a copy (phones die). (4) The map is updated annually — check the date on the PDF.

title: Citymapper Madrid (Transit App)
summary: Best transit app for Madrid. Real-time Metro, bus, Cercanías, BiciMAD, Uber, taxi. Free. Shows which Metro car to board.
content: Citymapper (free app, English) is the best transit app for Madrid — better than Google Maps for public transport. FEATURES: (1) Real-time arrivals for Metro, EMT buses, and Cercanías. (2) Compares all transport options: fastest, cheapest, fewest transfers. (3) Shows which Metro car to board for fastest exit (unique feature). (4) Includes BiciMAD (bike share), Uber, Cabify, and taxi options. (5) Live disruption alerts. (6) Offline mode for previously searched routes. (7) 'Get off' alarm — tells you when your stop is coming. HOW TO USE: (1) Download the app (iOS/Android). (2) Set your location to Madrid. (3) Enter your destination. (4) Citymapper shows all options with real-time arrivals and total journey time. (5) Choose your preferred option and follow the step-by-step directions. VS GOOGLE MAPS: Citymapper is better for transit (more accurate real-time, better route comparison, Metro car recommendations). Google Maps is better for walking and driving directions. Use both. VS EMT APP: Citymapper includes Metro and Cercanías (EMT app is buses only). TIPS: (1) Download the app before you arrive — it works immediately. (2) The 'get off' alarm is essential for tired travelers — set it and relax. (3) Citymapper's 'cheapest' option often suggests walking + Metro combinations that save €2-3. (4) The app works in 80+ cities worldwide — useful for your entire trip.

title: Retiro Park Map & Self-Guided Walk
summary: Free map of Retiro Park — 118 hectares. Shows lake, Crystal Palace, Rose Garden, fountains, playgrounds. Printable PDF + app.
content: Retiro Park map and self-guided walking route. THE MAP: Available free at: (1) The park's main entrance (Puerta de Alcalá side) — printed paper map. (2) esMadrid.com — downloadable PDF. (3) The esMadrid app — interactive GPS map. SELF-GUIDED WALK (2 hours, 4km): Start at Puerta de Alcalá entrance → (1) Walk down the Paseo de la Argentina (Statue Walk) — statues of Spanish kings. → (2) Estanque Grande (rowing lake) — rent a boat (€6/45 min) or just admire the Alfonso XII monument. → (3) Palacio de Cristal (Crystal Palace) — 1887 glass pavilion, free art exhibitions. → (4) Rosaleda (Rose Garden) — 4,000+ roses, best May-June. → (5) Fuente del Ángel Caído (Fountain of the Fallen Angel) — world's only public Lucifer statue. → (6) Parterre (French garden) — geometric hedges and fountains. → (7) Exit at Puerta de España (west side, near Atocha). PARK FACILITIES ON THE MAP: (1) 3 children's playgrounds (marked with swing icons). (2) 2 cafés (Pabellón del Jardín, near the lake; Florida Park, north entrance). (3) BiciMAD station (bike rental). (4) Public toilets (5 locations, marked). (5) Drinking fountains (10+ locations). (6) Exercise equipment (free, near the north entrance). TIPS: (1) The park is huge — a map is essential if you want to find specific sights. (2) Pick up the free paper map at the main entrance. (3) The Crystal Palace sometimes has long lines — check the map for alternative exits. (4) For jogging: the perimeter is 4.5km, flat, and marked. (5) The Rose Garden is best in May-June — check the map for the exact location (southeast corner).

title: Accessibility & Disabled Access in Madrid
summary: Madrid is increasingly accessible: all Metro stations have elevators (99%), most museums are wheelchair-friendly, adapted transport available.
content: Madrid has made significant accessibility improvements in recent years. METRO: 99% of stations have elevators (the few without are being upgraded). Look for the wheelchair symbol on Metro maps. All buses are wheelchair accessible with ramps. MUSEUMS: Prado, Reina Sofía, Thyssen, Royal Palace all fully wheelchair accessible with free entry for disabled visitors + companion. Adapted tours available. ATTRACTIONS: Retiro Park — flat, paved paths, accessible rowboats. Madrid Río — fully accessible. Temple of Debod — limited (steps). Plaza Mayor — flat and accessible. Gran Vía — wide sidewalks, mostly accessible. ACCOMMODATION: Most 4-5 star hotels have accessible rooms. Book directly and specify needs. HOSTELS: Limited — ask ahead. TRANSPORT: (1) Metro — accessible at 99% of stations. (2) EMT buses — all wheelchair accessible. (3) Taxis — some adapted taxis available, call Radio Taxi 91 547 82 00 and request 'taxi adaptado'. (4) Uber/Cabify — can request wheelchair-accessible vehicles. SERVICES: (1) ONCE (Spanish National Organization for the Blind) — guide dog access everywhere. (2) Spanish Sign Language interpreters — available at major museums, book ahead. (3) Mobility scooters — rent at Farmacias or medical supply stores. TIPS: (1) The official accessibility guide is at esmadrid.com/en/accessibility. (2) Most museums have tactile tours for visually impaired visitors. (3) Cobblestone streets in old town (La Latina, Lavapiés) can be challenging for wheelchairs. (4) Retiro Park is the most accessible green space.

title: Street Art & Graffiti in Madrid
summary: Madrid has a vibrant street art scene. Lavapiés is the epicenter. Guided tours, self-guided walks, annual festival (Urvanity). Free.
content: Madrid is one of Europe's best street art cities. WHERE TO SEE IT: (1) Lavapiés — the epicenter. Calle de la Argumosa, Calle del Casino, and side streets are covered in murals. The 'CaixaForum' vertical garden on Paseo del Prado is a landmark. (2) Malasaña — smaller pieces, paste-ups, and stickers around Calle del Pez and Calle del Espíritu Santo. (3) Tabacalera (Calle de la Embajadores 53) — former tobacco factory, now a cultural center with constantly changing street art. Free entry. (4) La Latina — murals around Plaza de la Cebada and Mercado de la Cebada. (5) Calle de la Mira (El Rastro area) — known for its murals. GUIDED TOURS: (1) Madrid Street Art Tour (€15, 2 hours, English) — covers Lavapiés and Tabacalera. Book at streetarttourmadrid.com. (2) Self-guided: download the 'Madrid Street Art' map from google.com/maps. FESTIVALS: (1) Urvanity Art (February-March) — international street art fair at COAM. (2) Muros de Tabacalera — ongoing mural program at Tabacalera. ARTISTS TO KNOW: (1) Okuda — colorful geometric animals (look for his murals in Lavapiés). (2) Suso33 — distinctive portrait style. (3) Zosen — cartoonish characters. (4) El Niño de las Pinturas — pioneer. TIPS: (1) Street art changes constantly — what's there today may be painted over tomorrow. (2) The best time to photograph is morning (good light, fewer people). (3) Tabacalera is free and open Tue-Sun 11am-8pm. (4) Respect the art — don't touch or deface.

title: Barrio de las Letras (Literary Quarter)
summary: Madrid's literary neighborhood where Cervantes, Lope de Vega, and Quevedo lived. Golden Age quotes on the streets. Plaza de Santa Ana.
content: Barrio de las Letras (Literary Quarter, also called Huertas) is the neighborhood east of Puerta del Sol, between Paseo del Prado and Calle de Atocha. In the 16th-17th centuries, Spain's Golden Age writers lived here: Cervantes (author of Don Quixote), Lope de Vega, Quevedo, Góngora, Tirso de Molina. WHAT TO SEE: (1) Golden Age quotes — famous literary quotes are embedded in the sidewalk in golden letters along Calle de las Huertas. Walk slowly and read them. (2) Cervantes' house (Calle de la León 8, now the Cervantes Institute) — not the original, but a reconstruction. (3) Lope de Vega's house (Calle de Cervantes 11) — the actual house where he lived 1610-1635. Small museum, €5. (4) Plaza de Santa Ana — the neighborhood's main square, lined with café terraces. The Hotel Reina Victoria (now ME Madrid) on the corner was where bullfighters stayed. (5) Congreso de los Diputados (Carrera de San Jerónimo) — Spain's Parliament, at the west end of the neighborhood. (6) Ateneo de Madrid (Calle del Prado 21) — historic cultural institution, library, literary events. LITERARY WALK: Start at Plaza de Santa Ana → Calle de las Huertas (read the quotes) → Lope de Vega's house → Cervantes' house → Plaza de las Cortes (Parliament). Allow 1-2 hours. TIPS: (1) The golden quotes on Calle de las Huertas are the highlight — don't rush. (2) Plaza de Santa Ana is great for a drink — but touristy. (3) The neighborhood is also called 'Huertas' (orchards) — both names refer to the same area. (4) Best at evening when the cafés spill onto the street.

title: Coffee Culture & Best Cafés in Madrid
summary: Third-wave coffee in Madrid: Toma Café, Bouteclare, HanSo. Plus traditional cafés: Café Comercial, Café Gijón. €1.50-4/coffee.
content: Madrid's coffee scene has exploded in the last 10 years. Traditional Spanish coffee (torrefacto, dark roasted with sugar) is still common, but specialty coffee is everywhere. SPECIALTY COFFEE SHOPS: (1) Toma Café (Calle de la Espíritu Santo 3, Malasaña) — the pioneer. Single-origin beans, flat whites, filter coffee. €2.50-4. The place that started Madrid's specialty coffee revolution. (2) Bouteclare (Calle de la Luna 5, Malasaña) — beautiful interior, excellent espresso. €2.50-4. (3) HanSo Café (Calle de la San Andrés 38, Malasaña) — Korean-owned, minimalist design, great filter. €2.50-4. (4) Momoka (Calle de la San Vicente Ferrer 28, Malasaña) — specialty coffee + cakes. (5) Accent Coffee (Calle de la Alonso Martínez 9) — newer, excellent flat whites. (6) Blue Bottle (Calle Gran Vía 28) — US chain, but reliable. TRADITIONAL CAFÉS: (1) Café Comercial (Calle de la Glorieta de Bilbao 7) — Madrid's oldest café (1887, reopened 2017). Beautiful modernist interior. €1.80 coffee. (2) Café Gijón (Paseo de Recoletos 21) — historic literary café (1905). Hemingway and Orson Welles drank here. €2.50. (3) Café del Jardín (inside the Egyptian Embassy garden, Calle de la Vicuña 6) — secret garden café. €2. HOW TO ORDER IN SPAIN: 'Café con leche' (coffee with milk, like a latte). 'Cortado' (espresso with a splash of milk). 'Café solo' (espresso, no milk). 'Carajillo' (espresso with brandy/whisky — a Spanish tradition). 'Café americano' (espresso with extra water — weaker). TIPS: (1) Spanish breakfast is coffee + a pastry (€2-3 total at a traditional bar). (2) Stand at the bar — it's cheaper than sitting at a table. (3) Specialty coffee shops are in Malasaña and Chueca — not in the tourist center. (4) 'Toma Café' is the must-visit for coffee lovers.

title: Public Toilets in Madrid — Where to Find Them
summary: Free public toilets are rare. Use: department stores (El Corte Inglés), museums, libraries, Metro stations, cafés (buy a coffee). €0-0.50.
content: Public toilets in Madrid — a practical guide. THE REALITY: Madrid has very few free public toilets on the street. You need to know where to find them. FREE OPTIONS: (1) El Corte Inglés (Castellana 53, Goya, Preciados) — clean, free toilets on every floor. The best option. (2) Museums — Prado, Reina Sofía, Thyssen, Royal Palace all have free toilets (but you need a ticket or go during free hours). (3) Public libraries (Bibliotecas Públicas) — 26 locations, free toilets, no ticket needed. (4) Metro stations — some larger stations (Sol, Atocha, Chamartín) have toilets, €0.50-1. (5) Madrid City Hall (Plaza de Cibeles, CentroCentro building) — free public toilets in the lobby. (6) Matadero Madrid (Plaza de Legazpi) — free. (7) Atocha Station — free toilets near the tropical garden. PAID OPTIONS: (1) Public toilet kiosks on Gran Vía and near Plaza Mayor — €0.50-1. (2) Some parks (Retiro) have toilets near the lake — €0.50. CAFÉ STRATEGY: The most reliable strategy is to buy a coffee (€1.50-2) at any café/bar and use their toilet. Spanish cafés don't mind if you use the toilet after buying something. TIPS: (1) Always carry €0.50 in coins for paid toilets. (2) El Corte Inglés is your best friend — clean, free, multiple locations. (3) Department stores and shopping centers always have free toilets. (4) McDonald's and Starbucks have toilets but may require a purchase or code from receipt. (5) Plan ahead — don't wait until you're desperate. (6) Hand sanitizer is useful — not all public toilets have soap.

title: Best Photography Spots in Madrid
summary: Top photo spots: Temple of Debod sunset, Gran Vía rooftop, Retiro Crystal Palace, Plaza Mayor at night, Alcalá arch. Free.
content: Best photography spots in Madrid. SUNSET/GOLDEN HOUR: (1) Temple of Debod (Parque del Oeste) — the iconic sunset shot. Egyptian temple silhouette against orange sky. Arrive 45 min before sunset. Free. (2) Círculo de Bellas Artes rooftop (Calle Alcalá 42) — €5, 360° views of Gran Vía. Best for blue hour. (3) Riu Plaza España rooftop (Gran Vía 137) — €10, highest viewpoint in Madrid. Great for sunset + cityscape. ARCHITECTURE: (4) Plaza Mayor — best at night when lit up, or early morning when empty. (5) Gran Vía from above — photograph from the Círculo de Bellas Artes rooftop or Hotel Riu. (6) Puerta de Alcalá — best from Plaza de la Independencia at golden hour. (7) Crystal Palace (Retiro Park) — light through the glass, reflections in the pond. Best mid-morning. (8) Atocha tropical garden — the old station hall with 4,000 plants. Atmospheric. STREET/LIFESTYLE: (9) El Rastro flea market (Sundays) — colorful, crowded, authentic Madrid. (10) Lavapiés — multicultural street life, colorful buildings. (11) Malasaña — hipster cafés, vintage shops, street art. (12) Mercado de San Miguel — food close-ups, bustling atmosphere. HIDDEN GEMS: (13) Plaza de la Villa — medieval square, few tourists. (14) Estación de Chamberí (Andén 0) — preserved 1919 Metro station, beautiful tile work. Free. (15) Jardines de Sabatini — formal gardens with Royal Palace backdrop. Best at sunset. TIPS: (1) Golden hour in Madrid: 7-8:30pm summer, 5-6pm winter. (2) Blue hour: 8:30-9pm summer, 6-6:30pm winter. (3) Tripods allowed outdoors but not inside museums. (4) Drone photography: regulated in Madrid city center — check AESA rules. (5) People photography: Spaniards are generally fine being photographed in public, but always ask for portraits. (6) Best Instagram spot: Temple of Debod at sunset — it's the Madrid equivalent of the Eiffel Tower.

title: Madrid for Digital Nomads
summary: Madrid is a top digital nomad destination. Coworking, fast WiFi, Ley de Startups (24% flat tax), great lifestyle. €1,500-2,500/month.
content: Madrid for digital nomads. WHY MADRID: (1) Fast WiFi everywhere (fiber 600Mbps+ standard). (2) Ley de Startups (2023) — special 24% flat tax rate for 5 years for remote workers moving to Spain. (3) Lower cost of living than London, Paris, Amsterdam. (4) Excellent public transport. (5) 300+ sunny days. (6) Thriving expat community. (7) Great food and nightlife. COSTS: Accommodation €600-1,200/month (room in shared flat to 1-bedroom). Coworking €120-250/month. Food €300-500/month. Transport €54.60/month (Metro pass). Total: €1,500-2,500/month. VISAS: (1) Non-lucrative visa — for retirees and remote workers with foreign income. No Spanish work allowed. Renewable. (2) Ley de Startups visa — for digital nomads and startup founders. 24% flat tax. Must work remotely for non-Spanish clients. (3) Student visa — if enrolled in a Spanish course. Allows 20 hours/week work. COWORKING: See our Coworking Spaces record for the full list. Top: WeWork (3 locations), Impact Hub, TNT Coworking. Day passes €20-30. WIFI: (1) Coworking spaces — fastest (600Mbps+). (2) Cafés — variable (ask for the WiFi password with your coffee). (3) Public WiFi — 'Madrid_Free_WiFi' (600+ hotspots, 2-hour sessions). (4) Mobile data — €20-30/month for 50GB+ (Vodafone, Orange, Lowi). NEIGHBORHOODS FOR NOMADS: (1) Malasaña — hipster, coworking, cafés, nightlife. Best for social nomads. (2) Chamberí — quiet, residential, good coworking. Best for focus. (3) Lavapiés — cheap, multicultural, artistic. Best for budget. (4) Salamanca — upscale, clean, business-oriented. Best for professionals. COMMUNITY: (1) Nomad List Madrid — active Slack group. (2) Madrid Digital Nomads (Meetup) — weekly meetups. (3) Impact Hub events — networking. (4) Intercambios (language exchanges) — great for meeting locals. TIPS: (1) The Ley de Startups visa is the best option — 24% flat tax for 5 years. Apply at a Spanish consulate. (2) Get a Spanish bank account (N26 or Revolut for quick setup). (3) Get a NIE (foreigner ID) — needed for everything. (4) Join 'Digital Nomads Madrid' on Facebook — 5,000+ members. (5) Summer (July-August) is too hot to work outdoors — coworking with AC is essential.

title: Spanish Food Basics — What to Know
summary: Olive oil, jamón, tortilla, gazpacho, paella, churros. What to try, what to order, what to avoid. The food primer for visitors.
content: Spanish food basics for visitors. THE BIG 10 TO TRY: (1) Jamón ibérico — cured ham from free-range Iberian pigs. Look for 'bellota' (acorn-fed) — the best. €5-15/plate. (2) Tortilla de patatas — Spanish potato omelet. Every bar makes one. €2-4/slice. (3) Gazpacho — cold tomato soup (summer). €4-6. (4) Paella — rice dish from Valencia (NOT Madrid, but everywhere). €12-20. (5) Croquetas — deep-fried bechamel fritters (jamón, chicken, cod). €2-3 for 2. (6) Patatas bravas — fried potatoes with spicy sauce. €3-5. (7) Pulpo a la gallega — Galician-style octopus with paprika. €8-12. (8) Cocido madrileño — Madrid's chickpea stew (winter). €15-22. (9) Churros con chocolate — fried dough with thick chocolate. €4.50. (10) Tinto de verano — red wine with lemon soda (like sangria but better). €2-4. WHAT TO AVOID: (1) Paella with chorizo — authentic paella never has chorizo (that's a tourist version). (2) Sangria — most Spaniards drink 'tinto de verano' instead (simpler, cheaper, better). (3) Restaurants with pictures of food outside — tourist traps. (4) Eating on Plaza Mayor or Puerta del Sol — overpriced and mediocre. (5) 'Menú del día' in tourist areas — go 2 streets away for better food at half the price. TAPAS ETIQUETTE: (1) Tapas are NOT free in Madrid (unlike Granada). You order and pay. (2) 'Ración' = full plate (share with 2-3 people). 'Media ración' = half. 'Tapa' = small portion. (3) Order 2-3 tapas per person for a meal. (4) Sharing is normal — order different dishes and pass them around. (5) Bread is not free — you'll be charged €1-2 if they bring it. Say 'sin pan' if you don't want it. OLIVE OIL: Spain produces 50% of the world's olive oil. 'Virgen extra' (extra virgin) is the best. Use it for everything — drizzling on bread, salads, cooking. A good bottle costs €5-10 in the supermarket. SPANISH MEALTIMES: See our Spanish Culture record — lunch is 2-3:30pm, dinner is 9-11pm. Don't try to eat dinner at 6pm — restaurants are closed.

title: Madrid Nightlife Etiquette & Tips
summary: How Madrid nightlife works: late start (11pm+), pre-game in parks, club peak 3-4am, dress codes, tipping, safety. Essential reading.
content: Madrid nightlife etiquette and practical tips. THE SCHEDULE: Madrid nightlife starts LATE. Bars fill at 11pm. Clubs at 1:30am. Peak 3-4am. Most clubs close 6am (just when Metro opens). Don't arrive at a club before 1am — you'll be the only one there. PRE-GAME (BOTELLÓN): Spaniards drink in parks/plazas before going to bars (to save money — club drinks are €10-15). Popular spots: Templo de Debod, Madrid Río, Plaza de España. Technically illegal but tolerated. Bring your own drinks from the supermarket. DRESS CODE: (1) Bars — casual (jeans, t-shirts OK). (2) Cocktail bars — smart casual. (3) Hotel rooftops — smart casual (no sportswear, no flip-flops). (4) Clubs — smart casual to dressy (no sportswear, no shorts, no flip-flops). (5) LGBTQ+ clubs — more flexible/creative. TIPPING: Not expected at bars. If you order a round, sometimes the bartender gives you a free snack (tapita) or a free shot. ORDERING DRINKS: (1) 'Una caña' — small draft beer (€2-3). The standard order. (2) 'Un tubo' — tall draft beer (€3-4). (3) 'Un tinto de verano' — red wine + lemon soda (€2-4). Better than sangria. (4) 'Un cubata' — rum + cola or other spirit + mixer (€6-10). (5) 'Un vermú' — vermouth on tap (€2-4). SAFETY: (1) Watch your drink — never leave it unattended. (2) Pickpockets work in crowded bars and clubs. Keep your phone in a front pocket. (3) Don't accept drinks from strangers (standard advice everywhere). (4) Use PideTaxi or FreeNow apps to get home — don't walk alone at 4am. (5) Metro runs until 1:30am (2:30am Fri/Sat), then night buses from Cibeles. GETTING HOME: (1) Metro starts at 6am — perfect timing for club exit. (2) Night buses (Búhos) from Cibeles — €1.50. (3) Taxi — €10-15 from center to most neighborhoods. (4) Uber/Cabify — watch for surge pricing (2-3x after 3am). TIPS: (1) Pace yourself — Madrid nights are 6+ hours. (2) Eat a kebab or churros at 4am — it's tradition. (3) The Spanish don't get drunk like Northern Europeans — moderate drinking is the norm. (4) If you're invited to someone's house ('previa'), bring drinks. (5) Spanish people go out in groups — join a group, don't go alone.

title: The Official Madrid Guide Booklet (with Madrid Card)
summary: The booklet that comes with the Madrid Card. 150+ pages: itineraries, museums, restaurants, maps, discounts. Available as free PDF.
content: The Official Madrid Guide is the booklet that accompanies the Madrid City Card (and is also available as a free PDF download). CONTENTS (150+ pages): (1) Madrid itineraries — 1-day, 2-day, 3-day, and family itineraries with maps. (2) Museums and monuments — descriptions, hours, admission prices, and how to get there for 50+ attractions. (3) Restaurants and gastronomy — curated restaurant recommendations by neighborhood and budget. (4) Shopping guide — by neighborhood (Gran Vía, Salamanca, Malasaña). (5) Nightlife — bars, clubs, flamenco, theaters. (6) Parks and gardens — Retiro, Casa de Campo, Madrid Río, Quinta de los Molinos. (7) Day trips — Toledo, Segovia, El Escorial, Aranjuez, Alcalá de Henares. (8) Practical info — transport, emergency numbers, embassies, hospitals, pharmacies. (9) Maps — city center, Metro, bus, neighborhoods. (10) Discount coupons — 20-50% off at selected restaurants, shops, and attractions. HOW TO GET IT: (1) Free PDF download at esmadrid.com/en/madrid-guide (27MB, 150+ pages). (2) Paper copy at any Tourist Office (Plaza Mayor, airport, Atocha) — free. (3) Included with the Madrid City Card purchase (€48-108). (4) At hotels — most hotel receptions have copies. HOW WE COMPARE: Our Madrid Insider website covers ALL the same topics as the guidebook, but: (1) We're updated continuously (the PDF is annual). (2) We have 286 records vs the guidebook's ~150 listings. (3) We have live weather, real-time transit alerts, and search. (4) We link directly to official websites. (5) We have English availability badges (EN★, EN, 🗝️ MI). (6) We have tips on every subcategory page. WHAT THE GUIDEBOOK HAS THAT WE DON'T: (1) Beautiful photos (we're text-focused). (2) Discount coupons. (3) Offline access (a PDF works without internet). WHAT WE HAVE THAT THE GUIDEBOOK DOESN'T: (1) 4-tier English availability system. (2) Sub-subcategory grouping (160 groups). (3) Bidirectional related-records linking. (4) Live weather API. (5) Dynamic per-page SEO. (6) Search across all 286 records. (7) Updated daily (not annually). (8) Spanish-only resource coverage (🗝️ MI badge). TIPS: (1) Download the PDF guidebook AND bookmark Madrid Insider — use both. (2) The guidebook is great for planning before your trip (print it). (3) Madrid Insider is better during your trip (search, live data, always current). (4) The guidebook has discount coupons worth €20-50 if you visit multiple attractions. (5) If you buy the Madrid City Card (€48+), the guidebook comes free.

title: Madrid Metropolitan (English Magazine)
summary: Madrid's only English-language print + online magazine. News, what's on, interviews, features. Active. Free online.
content: Madrid Metropolitan (madridmetropolitan.com) is Madrid's only active English-language print and online magazine. It's Spain's leading English-language news portal, covering Madrid and the wider region. WHAT THEY COVER: (1) Local news — Madrid politics, city events, infrastructure. (2) What's on — events, exhibitions, concerts, festivals. (3) Interviews — expats, entrepreneurs, artists, politicians. (4) Features — neighborhood guides, food reviews, lifestyle. (5) Expat guides — practical advice on NIE, healthcare, housing, taxes. (6) Business — startup scene, real estate, economy. HISTORY: The Madrid Metropolitan has been publishing for over 20 years, making it one of Madrid's longest-running English-language publications. It succeeded earlier publications like The Broadsheet and In Madrid magazine. DISTRIBUTION: (1) Online at madridmetropolitan.com (free, updated daily). (2) Print edition (monthly, distributed at hotels, embassies, restaurants, and British Council). (3) Email newsletter (free, weekly). SECTIONS: News, Expat Guide, What's On, Food & Drink, Lifestyle, Business, Sport, Opinion. HOW IT COMPARES TO MADRID INSIDER: Madrid Metropolitan focuses on NEWS (daily updates, current events), while Madrid Insider focuses on RESOURCES (permanent directory, official links, structured info). They complement each other — use Madrid Metropolitan for 'what's happening this week' and Madrid Insider for 'how to do X in Madrid'. TIPS: (1) Sign up for their weekly email newsletter — best English-language events roundup. (2) Their 'Expat Guide' section has practical advice similar to our Living category. (3) Follow @MadridMetropol on Twitter for breaking news. (4) Pick up the print edition at the British Council (Calle del General Martínez Campos 31) or major hotels.

title: The Broadsheet (Historical English Magazine)
summary: Madrid's longest-established English-language magazine. Published 6x/year. Covered fashion, travel, real estate, design. Now ceased print.
content: The Broadsheet was Madrid's longest-established English-language magazine, published six times a year. It covered fashion, travel, real estate, and design for the English-speaking community in Madrid. HISTORY: The Broadsheet was founded in the 1980s (some sources say earlier) and served as the primary English-language lifestyle publication for Madrid's growing expat community. It was distributed at hotels, embassies, V.O. cinemas, Irish pubs, and the British Council. CONTENT: The magazine focused on upscale lifestyle — fashion spreads, travel features (Spanish destinations), real estate listings (upscale apartments in Salamanca and La Moraleja), interior design, restaurant reviews, and cultural events. It was known for its high production quality (glossy, full-color) and sophisticated tone. STATUS: The Broadsheet appears to have ceased print publication. Its digital presence is minimal. The void it left has been filled by: (1) Madrid Metropolitan (news and features, online + print). (2) Essential Magazine (now focused on Marbella, was previously in Madrid). (3) Madrid No Frills (blog, authentic/cheap). (4) Madrid Insider (our site — permanent resource directory). LEGACY: The Broadsheet was mentioned in numerous Madrid expat guides as a key resource. It was referenced in Syracuse University's Madrid visitor guide as 'Madrid's longest established English-language magazine.' The magazine played an important role in the English-speaking community during the 1990s-2000s, before the internet made online publications dominant. TIPS: (1) The Broadsheet is no longer publishing — don't look for current copies. (2) For modern English-language Madrid content, see our Madrid Metropolitan, El País English, and The Local Spain records. (3) Back issues may be available at the National Library of Spain (Biblioteca Nacional).

title: In Madrid Magazine (Historical)
summary: Monthly English-language newspaper for Madrid expats and students. Distributed free at hotels, V.O. cinemas, Irish pubs. Now ceased.
content: In Madrid (in-madrid.com) was a monthly English-language newspaper/magazine geared toward expats and students in Madrid. It was distributed free at hotels, original-version (V.O.) cinemas, Irish pubs, English bookstores, and the British Council. HISTORY: In Madrid published for over 15 years, serving as a key resource for the English-speaking community in Madrid. It was mentioned in TripAdvisor forums and expat guides as 'a very good source of information for the English speaking community.' CONTENT: (1) Local news and events in English. (2) Classified ads (housing, jobs, services). (3) Restaurant and bar reviews. (4) Cultural events (exhibitions, concerts, theater). (5) Practical advice for expats (NIE, healthcare, banking). (6) Community announcements. STATUS: In Madrid appears to have ceased publication. The website (in-madrid.com) is no longer active. The void has been filled by Madrid Metropolitan (online + print), El País English (daily news), and various blogs. LEGACY: In Madrid was an important bridge for the English-speaking community in Madrid during the pre-internet and early-internet era. Before Google Translate and smartphones, it was often the only way for non-Spanish speakers to find out what was happening in the city. TIPS: (1) In Madrid is no longer publishing — don't look for current copies. (2) For modern English-language Madrid content, use Madrid Metropolitan, El País English, The Local Spain, and Madrid Insider (our site). (3) The name 'In Madrid' may live on as a brand — check if it has been revived.

title: History of English-Language Publishing in Madrid
summary: Complete history of English-language publications in Madrid: The Standard (1992), The Broadsheet, In Madrid, Essential Magazine, Madrid Metropolitan, and now Madrid Insider.
content: History of English-language publishing in Madrid. THE TIMELINE: (1) THE STANDARD (1992) — A serious English-language daily newspaper launched in Madrid. Priced at 50 pesetas (about €0.30). It was a 'European' style newspaper. Short-lived but ambitious. (2) THE BROADSHEET (1980s-2010s?) — Madrid's longest-established English-language magazine. Published 6 times a year. Covered fashion, travel, real estate, design. Upscale, glossy, sophisticated. Distributed at hotels, embassies, V.O. cinemas. Now ceased. (3) IN MADRID (2000s-2010s) — Monthly English-language newspaper for expats and students. Free distribution at hotels, Irish pubs, V.O. cinemas, English bookstores. Covered local news, classifieds, events, practical advice. Now ceased. (4) ESSENTIAL MAGAZINE (2000s-2010s) — Free monthly lifestyle magazine. Distributed in Madrid and Barcelona. Covered lifestyle, food, culture, expat advice. Now appears focused on Marbella (essentialmagazine.com redirects to Essential Marbella). (5) MADRID METROPOLITAN (2000s-present) — Madrid's only ACTIVE English-language print + online magazine. Covers news, what's on, interviews, features. Print edition distributed monthly. Online at madridmetropolitan.com. (6) MADRID NO FRILLS (2010s-present) — Independent blog. Authentic cheap eats, hidden gems, off-the-beaten-path recommendations. English. Active at madridnofrills.com. (7) MADRID INSIDER (2026-present) — Our site. A permanent resource directory with 296 records, official links, structured sections, live weather, and 4-tier English availability badges. Not a magazine — a directory. THE PATTERN: English-language publishing in Madrid has gone through 3 phases: (1) Print era (1990s-2000s) — The Standard, The Broadsheet, In Madrid. Print was the only option. Distribution was physical (hotels, pubs, cinemas). (2) Transition era (2010s) — Essential Magazine, Madrid Metropolitan. Both print and online. Print shrinks, online grows. (3) Digital era (2020s-present) — Madrid No Frills (blog), Madrid Insider (directory). Digital-only, searchable, always current. WHO FILLS THE VOID NOW: (1) Daily news: El País English, The Local Spain. (2) Magazine/features: Madrid Metropolitan. (3) Blog/authentic: Madrid No Frills. (4) Directory/resources: Madrid Insider (our site). (5) Radio: RNE 5 (BBC World Service overnight). (6) Community: Facebook groups, Meetup, WhatsApp groups. TIPS: (1) Madrid Metropolitan is the direct successor to The Broadsheet and In Madrid. (2) For historical research, the National Library of Spain (Biblioteca Nacional) may have archives. (3) The Syracuse University Madrid visitor guide (2019) references both The Broadsheet and In Madrid as resources.

title: MultiMadrid.com (Historical — Virtual Madrid Tours)
summary: Defunct Madrid website (2001-2015). Featured live Plaza Mayor webcam, 360° panoramas, virtual tours of 40+ Madrid landmarks, events, and Spain forums.
content: MultiMadrid.com was a pioneering English-language Madrid website created by Jeremy (jeremy@multimadrid.com). It was active from approximately 2001 to 2015. TAGLINE: 'You Never Have to Leave Madrid!' — 'The ultimate virtual tour of Madrid.' WHAT IT OFFERED: (1) Live Plaza Mayor Webcam — one of the first live webcams in Madrid, showing the iconic Plaza Mayor in real time. (2) 360° Panoramic Virtual Tours — interactive 360-degree panoramas of 40+ Madrid landmarks, including Plaza Mayor, Puerta del Sol, Royal Palace, Prado, Temple of Debod, Atocha Station, El Rastro, and more. Users could 'walk' through Madrid virtually. (3) Madrid Photography — extensive photo galleries of Madrid monuments, plazas, fountains, and streets. (4) Madrid Videos — video clips of Madrid events and landmarks. (5) Events Calendar — San Isidro, Semana Santa, Carnavales, La Vuelta cycling, Madrid Marathon, Book Fair, Dos de Mayo. (6) Spain Forums — active message board (spaintalk.multimadrid.org) with forums on Madrid travel, living in Spain, accommodation, and classifieds. (7) Spain Chatrooms — real-time chat for Spain enthusiasts. (8) Spain Classifieds — housing, jobs, services. (9) Screensavers and E-cards — Madrid-themed digital products. (10) Guestbook — visitor comments. LANDMARKS COVERED (40+): Plaza Mayor, Puerta del Sol, Plaza de la Villa, Plaza de España, Plaza de Oriente, Plaza de Cibeles, Plaza de Colón, El Prado, Puerta de Alcalá, Palacio Real, Temple of Debod, Post Office (Correos), Jardines Sabatini, La Almudena, Atocha Station, Congress Building, El Rastro, and more. EVENTS COVERED: San Isidro, Semana Santa, Carnavales, La Vuelta (cycling), Madrid Marathon, Book Fair, Dos de Mayo, International Poetry Day, National Stamp Fair. STATUS: The domain multimadrid.com now redirects to a blank page. The site appears to have ceased around 2015. The Wayback Machine has snapshots from 2005-2012. THE VOID: MultiMadrid.com was unique in its visual approach — it was the 'virtual tour' of Madrid before Google Street View existed. The 360° panoramas and live webcam were groundbreaking for their time. The Spain Forums (spaintalk.multimadrid.org) were an active community. WHO FILLS THE VOID NOW: (1) Virtual tours: Google Street View, Google Earth. (2) Live webcam: EarthCam ( Plaza Mayor), SkylineWebcams. (3) Madrid photography: Instagram (#madrid), Flickr. (4) Events: esMadrid.com, Madrid Metropolitan. (5) Forums: Reddit (r/Madrid), Facebook groups. (6) Classifieds: Idealista, InfoJobs. (7) Madrid directory: Madrid Insider (our site — 300 records). TIPS: (1) MultiMadrid.com is defunct — don't try to visit it. (2) The Wayback Machine has archived snapshots at web.archive.org/web/*/multimadrid.com. (3) The site's creator Jeremy was an early Madrid digital pioneer — his work deserves recognition.

title: MadridMan.com (English Madrid Guide)
summary: Long-running English-language Madrid guide by 'MadridMan'. Hotel reviews, restaurant picks, trip reports, Madrid links. Active at madridman.com.
content: MadridMan.com is a long-running English-language Madrid guide website created and maintained by 'MadridMan' (a passionate American Madrid enthusiast). The site has been active since at least 2000 and appears to still be operational at madridman.com. WHAT IT OFFERS: (1) Madrid Hotels — curated hotel recommendations with personal reviews. (2) Madrid Restaurants — restaurant picks with English-friendliness notes. MadridMan specifically notes which restaurants have English menus and English-speaking staff. (3) Madrid Trip Reports — detailed personal trip reports from MadridMan's multiple visits (2000, 2001, 2002, etc.). These are time-capsule views of Madrid through the years. (4) Favorite Madrid Links — comprehensive directory of Madrid-related websites, resources, and services. (5) Madrid Photos — personal photography from visits. (6) Spain Travel Tips — practical advice for Spain travelers. WHAT MAKES IT UNIQUE: (1) Personal voice — MadridMan writes from the perspective of a passionate repeat visitor, not a professional travel writer. (2) Long history — the site has been active for 20+ years, providing a historical record of how Madrid has changed. (3) English-friendliness focus — MadridMan specifically notes which restaurants and hotels accommodate English speakers, which aligns perfectly with our mission. (4) Detailed restaurant reviews — includes specifics like 'Spanish staff speaks English, most all speak some English. Reservations for lunch and especially dinner is absolutely necessary. Menus in Spanish and English.' STATUS: The site appears to still be active, though the design is dated (early-2000s HTML). The content is personal and opinionated — not a comprehensive directory like Madrid Insider, but a valuable personal perspective. HOW IT COMPARES TO MADRID INSIDER: MadridMan is a personal blog/trip report site with deep personal experience. Madrid Insider is a structured directory with 300 records, official links, and live data. They complement each other — MadridMan for personal recommendations and trip reports, Madrid Insider for comprehensive resource directory. TIPS: (1) Check MadridMan's restaurant page for personal recommendations with English-friendliness notes. (2) His trip reports are fascinating historical documents — read the 2000 trip report to see how Madrid has changed. (3) His 'Favorite Links' page is a time-capsule of early-2000s Madrid web resources. (4) The site design is old-school but the content is genuine and useful.

title: Semana Santa (Holy Week) in Madrid
summary: Madrid's Holy Week processions (March/April). 20+ brotherhoods parade through the center. Free. Less intense than Seville but still impressive.
content: Semana Santa (Holy Week) in Madrid is the week before Easter. Madrid's processions are less famous than Seville's but still impressive and more accessible (fewer crowds). WHEN: The week before Easter Sunday (March or April, varies by year). 2026 dates: March 28 – April 5. WHAT HAPPENS: 20+ religious brotherhoods (cofradías) parade through the streets of central Madrid, carrying elaborate floats (pasos) depicting scenes from the Passion of Christ. The processions are solemn, accompanied by drums and trumpets, and many participants wear traditional hooded robes (capirotes). KEY PROCESSIONS: (1) Viernes de Dolores (Friday before Palm Sunday) — opening procession, Plaza de la Villa. (2) Domingo de Ramos (Palm Sunday) — Palm Sunday procession from Iglesia de San Nicolás. (3) Jueves Santo (Holy Thursday) — the most important day, multiple processions in the evening. (4) Viernes Santo (Good Friday) — morning and evening processions, including the solemn 'Procesión del Silencio' (silent procession) at midnight. (5) Sábado Santo (Holy Saturday) — quiet day, no processions. (6) Domingo de Resurrección (Easter Sunday) — joyful closing procession. WHERE TO WATCH: (1) Plaza Mayor — processions pass through here. (2) Calle de Bailén — near the Cathedral. (3) Plaza de la Villa — starting point for many processions. (4) Anywhere along the route — just stand on the sidewalk. SCHEDULE: Published annually at esmadrid.com/en/easter-week-madrid. Processions typically start at 7-8pm and last 2-4 hours. TIPS: (1) It's FREE — just show up and watch from the sidewalk. (2) Be respectful — this is a religious event, not a parade. Keep quiet during processions. (3) Dress modestly. (4) Arrive 30 min early for good viewing spots. (5) The midnight 'Procesión del Silencio' on Good Friday is the most atmospheric. (6) Madrid's Semana Santa is less crowded than Seville's — you can actually see the processions without fighting through crowds. (7) Many restaurants and shops close on Good Friday. (8) The floats are works of art — some date back to the 17th century.

title: Carnavales de Madrid (Carnival)
summary: Madrid's Carnival (February). Costumes, parades, street music, the 'Burial of the Sardine' on Ash Wednesday. Free. Less wild than Rio or Tenerife.
content: Carnavales de Madrid is the city's Carnival celebration, held in February (the week before Lent). Madrid's carnival is less famous than Rio, Tenerife, or Cadiz, but it's a fun, family-friendly celebration. WHEN: The week before Ash Wednesday (February, varies by year). 2026 dates: February 6-17. WHAT HAPPENS: (1) Opening speech (pregón) — Thursday evening, usually at Plaza de la Villa or Plaza Mayor. A celebrity gives the opening speech. (2) Costume parades — Saturday and Sunday, people in costumes parade through the center. (3) Street music — bands and performers in plazas. (4) Children's carnival — Sunday morning, kids' costume parade. (5) Gastronomy — special carnival foods (torrijas, huevos estrellados). (6) Burial of the Sardine (Entierro de la Sardina) — Ash Wednesday evening. THE BURIAL OF THE SARDINE: The most unique Madrid carnival event. On Ash Wednesday, a mock funeral procession marches through the streets, 'mourning' a sardine. People dress in black (widows' costumes), wail dramatically, and carry a coffin with a sardine inside. The procession ends at the Casa de Campo lake, where the sardine is 'buried' (thrown in the lake). The tradition dates to the 18th century and is both absurd and wonderful. WHERE: (1) Plaza Mayor — opening ceremony and events. (2) Calle de la Latina and La Latina neighborhood — main parade route. (3) Casa de Campo — Burial of the Sardine endpoint. (4) Various cultural centers — indoor events. SCHEDULE: Published at esmadrid.com/en/carnival-madrid. Most events are FREE. TIPS: (1) Wear a costume — even a simple mask or hat makes you part of the fun. (2) The Burial of the Sardine (Ash Wednesday evening) is the highlight — don't miss it. (3) Less crowded than other Madrid festivals — you can actually move around. (4) Family-friendly — kids love the costumes. (5) Many bars have special carnival menus and drinks. (6) Madrid's carnival is more low-key than Cadiz or Tenerife — if you want wild carnival, go to those cities.

title: Madrid Flatmate (Historical — Accommodation Service)
summary: Defunct Madrid accommodation service (2000s-2010s). Office at C/Canarias 28. Rooms, flats, baggage storage, cell phone rental, airport pickup. Partner of original Madrid Insider.
content: Madrid Flatmate (madridflatmate.com) was a Madrid-based accommodation service that operated from the 2000s through the early 2010s. It was a partner service of the original Madrid Insider website. WHAT THEY OFFERED: (1) Short and long-term accommodation — rooms in shared flats, studios, apartments, lofts. Primarily for Erasmus students, international students, and expats. (2) Baggage storage (consigna de equipajes) — left luggage service at their office. (3) Cell phone rental — for international visitors who needed a Spanish phone. (4) Airport pick-up service — met you at Barajas and brought you to your accommodation. (5) Tourist information — free info service at their office. OFFICE: C/Canarias Nº 28 - Local A, Madrid. Metro: Palos de la Frontera (Line 3). Phone: 91 530 34 82 / 686 20 86 13. TAGLINE: 'ALL THAT YOU NEED'. TARGET AUDIENCE: Erasmus students, international students at Complutense, Autónoma, Carlos III, Politécnica universities. Also served tourists and short-term visitors. FEATURES: (1) Featured on esMadrid TV (official Madrid tourism channel). (2) Physical office — not just a website, you could visit and get help in person. (3) 'Club Madrid Flatmate' — membership program with benefits. (4) Available rooms/flats listing on their website. (5) Multi-language service (English, Spanish). CONNECTION TO MADRID INSIDER: Madrid Flatmate was listed in the original Madrid Insider website's keywords (2005 snapshot) as a partner service. The original Madrid Insider directed visitors to Madrid Flatmate for accommodation needs. STATUS: The domain madridflatmate.com now redirects to a Linktree page for a coin collecting YouTube channel — completely repurposed. The service appears to have ceased operations sometime in the 2010s. WHO FILLS THE VOID NOW: (1) Room/flat search: Idealista, Fotocasa, Badi, Spotahome. (2) Baggage storage: Left luggage at Atocha/Chamartín/Barajas (see our Storage & Lockers records). (3) Cell phone rental: SIM cards (Vodafone, Orange) and eSIMs (Airalo). (4) Airport pickup: Uber, Cabify, Taxi (€30 flat rate), Metro L8. (5) Tourist info: Official Tourist Offices (Plaza Mayor), Madrid Insider (our site). LEGACY: Madrid Flatmate was one of the first one-stop-shop services for English-speaking arrivals in Madrid — combining accommodation, storage, phones, and airport pickup in one place. This 'all-in-one arrival service' concept is now split across multiple platforms and apps.

title: WhatMadrid.com (English Madrid Guide)
summary: Active English-language Madrid city guide. 67 sections covering sights, food, transport, neighborhoods, culture, day trips. Part of the 'What' travel guide series.
content: WhatMadrid.com is an active English-language Madrid city guide, part of the 'What' travel guide series covering Spain and Latin America. The site provides practical, tourist, and cultural information about Madrid. WHAT IT COVERS (67 sections): (1) About Madrid — history, geography, people. (2) City Guide — neighborhood-by-neighborhood orientation. (3) City Transport — Metro, buses, taxis, airport. (4) Culture & The Arts — museums, theaters, galleries. (5) Food in Madrid — restaurants, tapas, Spanish cuisine. (6) Hotels & Hostels — accommodation by area and budget. (7) Apartments — short-term and long-term rentals. (8) Flamenco in Madrid — tablaos and shows. (9) Bullfights — Las Ventas. (10) Festivals in Madrid — annual events calendar. (11) Embassies & Visas — practical info. (12) El Escorial & Valle de los Caídos — day trips. (13) Car Rental — driving in Madrid. (14) Flights to Madrid — airport info. (15) Cultural Centers — museums and arts venues. (16) Neighborhoods — Chueca, Malasaña, La Latina, Salamanca, and more. STRENGTHS: (1) Comprehensive — covers 67 topics (more than most online guides). (2) Well-organized — clear navigation by topic. (3) English-language throughout. (4) Includes practical info (embassies, transport, visas). (5) Covers day trips (El Escorial, Valle de los Caídos, Granada). HOW IT COMPARES TO MADRID INSIDER: WhatMadrid is a traditional travel guide format (articles/essays by topic). Madrid Insider is a structured directory (records with official links, search, sub-subcategories). WhatMadrid has more narrative content (longer articles); Madrid Insider has more structured data (tables, stats, sections, crossover links). They complement each other — WhatMadrid for reading/narrative, Madrid Insider for quick reference/official links. STATUS: Active at whatmadrid.com. The design is traditional (article-based) but the content appears current. TIPS: (1) Their neighborhood guides are good for getting oriented. (2) Their festivals page has a useful annual calendar. (3) Their El Escorial + Valle de los Caídos coverage is more detailed than most guides. (4) Use Madrid Insider for official links and structured info; use WhatMadrid for narrative context.

title: WelcomeToMadrid.org (Historical — Donald's Madrid Portal)
summary: Defunct English-language Madrid portal run by 'Donald'. Part of the early Madrid English web ecosystem with Madrid Insider, MultiMadrid, MadridMan, In Madrid, and Madrid Flatmate.
content: WelcomeToMadrid.org was an English-language Madrid portal run by 'Donald' (last name unknown). It was part of the early-2000s English-language Madrid web ecosystem. WHAT IT WAS: Based on Wayback Machine analysis (2007 snapshot), WelcomeToMadrid.org appears to have been a link directory/portal that connected the various English-language Madrid websites. The site's metadata listed all the known English Madrid resources as keywords: madridflatmate, multimadrid, madridman, madridinsider, in-madrid, aerocity — suggesting it served as a hub or portal for the English-speaking Madrid community. THE ECOSYSTEM: In the early 2000s, a small ecosystem of English-language Madrid websites existed: (1) Madrid Insider (madridinsider.com) — the original directory/list of lists. (2) MultiMadrid.com — virtual tours, webcam, forums. (3) MadridMan.com — personal guide, trip reports. (4) In Madrid — monthly English newspaper. (5) Madrid Flatmate — accommodation service. (6) WelcomeToMadrid.org — Donald's portal, connecting these sites. (7) The Broadsheet — print magazine. These sites cross-linked to each other and served different niches (directory, visual tours, personal guide, news, housing, portal). Donald's site appears to have been the 'connector' that linked them all together. STATUS: The domain welcometomadrid.org is no longer active. By 2012, it was a hosting placeholder. The .com variant was briefly a Spanish apartment rental site (2010). The .net variant was a Spanish WordPress blog (2016). None of these appear to be Donald's original site. LEGACY: WelcomeToMadrid.org represents the early-2000s era when English-language Madrid content was scattered across multiple small websites, each run by passionate individuals. These sites needed a portal to connect them — Donald's site served that role. Today, the need for such a portal is filled by search engines (Google), social media (Facebook groups), and comprehensive directories like Madrid Insider (our site — 306 records). WHO FILLS THE VOID: Madrid Insider (our site) now serves as the comprehensive directory that WelcomeToMadrid.org aspired to be — but with 306 records, official links, live weather, search, and structured sections. TIPS: (1) The domain is defunct — don't try to visit it. (2) For the current English-language Madrid ecosystem, see our History of English-Language Publishing in Madrid record. (3) The Wayback Machine has a 2007 snapshot at web.archive.org/web/20071213185307/http://www.welcometomadrid.org/.

title: Hot English Magazine (Madrid-Based English Learning)
summary: English learning magazine published from Madrid (Torrelodones). 260+ issues. Active at learnhotenglish.com. Print + digital + audio/video.
content: Hot English Magazine (learnhotenglish.com) is an English-language learning magazine published from Madrid. The company (Hot English Publishing Ltd) is based in Torrelodones, a suburb 30km northwest of Madrid. The magazine has been publishing since at least 2006 and is still active — issue 263 is the latest. WHAT IT IS: A monthly magazine for learning (or teaching) English as a second language. Tagline: 'The Fun & Easy Way To Learn Or Teach English.' It's designed for non-native English speakers worldwide — not specifically for Madrid expats, but it's produced in Madrid and has a Madrid connection. CONTENT: (1) Vocabulary — themed vocabulary (travel, business, idioms, phrasal verbs). (2) Reading — articles on culture, film, music, travel, news, business. (3) Listening — audio tracks accompanying each article. (4) Video — video content for visual learners. (5) Grammar — practical grammar in context. (6) Pronunciation — accent and pronunciation tips. (7) Skills booklet — additional exercises with each issue. FORMATS: (1) Print magazine — monthly, available by subscription. (2) Digital — via Pocketmags app. (3) Audio — MP3 downloads. (4) Video — online. (5) E-books — complete method packages. (6) Teacher resources — lesson plans, worksheets. HISTORY: Founded by Hot English Publishing Ltd. The company has 107 followers on LinkedIn. Originally at hotenglishmagazine.com (now redirects to learnhotenglish.com / learnhotenglishcorp.com). Based at Carrer Roser 65, 28250 Torrelodones, Madrid. MADRID CONNECTION: While the magazine is for English learners worldwide, it's produced in Madrid by a Madrid-based team. It represents Madrid's role as a hub for English-language education (Madrid has one of Europe's largest English teaching communities). The magazine may have been distributed at English academies and language schools in Madrid. HOW IT RELATES TO MADRID INSIDER: Hot English is for learning English (for Spanish speakers and others). Madrid Insider is for navigating Madrid in English (for English speakers). They're complementary — Hot English helps Spaniards learn English, Madrid Insider helps English speakers navigate Madrid. STATUS: Active at learnhotenglish.com (redirects to learnhotenglishcorp.com). 260+ issues published. Available by subscription (digital and print). TIPS: (1) Not specifically a Madrid guide — it's an English learning magazine produced in Madrid. (2) Useful if you're teaching English in Madrid (see our Teach English record) — the teacher resources are practical. (3) The magazine is popular in Spain, particularly in Madrid's large English academy market. (4) Available at some English bookstores in Madrid (see our Bookstores record). (5) The Torrelodones office is not open to the public.

title: Dave's ESL Cafe — Spain Forum (English Teaching Jobs)
summary: The #1 global ESL job board with a dedicated Spain forum. English teaching jobs in Madrid and Spain. Active. Free to browse, post, and search.
content: Dave's ESL Cafe (eslcafe.com) is the world's largest and longest-running ESL/EFL job board and community — founded in 1995 by Dave Sperling. It has a dedicated Spain forum (forums.eslcafe.com/job/viewforum.php?f=21) where English teaching jobs in Madrid and across Spain are posted. WHAT IT OFFERS: (1) Job board — schools and academies post teaching positions. Free to browse and apply. (2) Spain forum — active discussion board for English teachers in Spain. Questions about visas, academies, salaries, living costs, and teaching experiences. (3) International job board — jobs worldwide. (4) Teacher resources — lesson plans, activities, grammar guides. (5) Student resources — grammar lessons, idioms, phrasal verbs. THE SPAIN FORUM: The Spain section of Dave's ESL Cafe has been one of the primary online gathering places for English teachers in Spain since the late 1990s. Teachers share experiences about specific academies (which ones pay on time, which ones to avoid), discuss visa issues, and post job openings. HISTORY: Dave's ESL Cafe was founded in 1995 — making it one of the oldest still-active ESL websites. It predates LinkedIn, Indeed, and modern job boards. For years, it was THE place to find English teaching jobs abroad. THE IBERENGLISH CONNECTION: IberEnglish, run by Rebecca Gaspar (Director, based in Madrid at C/ Mundillo 3, 28029 Madrid), is a separate English academy and training company. Rebecca may have acquired or managed a Spain-specific ESL site or section at some point — the exact connection is unclear from public records, but IberEnglish is an active Madrid English academy. IBERENGLISH: (1) Active English academy in Madrid (Calle Amalia 9, and C/ Mundillo 3, 28029 Madrid). (2) Offers English classes for kids, teens, and adults. (3) Kids English Club (KEC) — 100% English summer camps. (4) Professional English training for businesses. (5) Director: Rebecca Gaspar (LinkedIn: linkedin.com/in/gasparrebecca). (6) Website: iberenglish.com (active). (7) Phone: 931 521 43. (8) Facebook: facebook.com/iberenglish (5.0 rating). HOW TO USE DAVE'S ESL CAFE FOR MADRID: (1) Browse the Spain forum for job postings. (2) Search for specific academy names to find teacher reviews. (3) Post questions about teaching in Madrid — the community is helpful. (4) Check the international job board for Spain-specific listings. (5) Use the teacher resources for lesson planning. TIPS: (1) Dave's ESL Cafe is free — no registration needed to browse. (2) The Spain forum is less active than it was in the 2000s (LinkedIn and Facebook groups have taken over), but it still has valuable historical posts. (3) For current Madrid teaching jobs, also check: InfoJobs, LinkedIn, Facebook groups ('English Teachers Madrid', 'Madrid Expats'). (4) IberEnglish is an active Madrid academy — contact them directly if you're looking for teaching work or English classes.

title: IberEnglish (Madrid English Academy)
summary: Active Madrid English academy run by Rebecca Gaspar. Classes for kids, teens, adults. Kids English Club summer camps. Calle Amalia 9, Madrid.
content: IberEnglish (iberenglish.com) is an active English academy in Madrid, run by director Rebecca Gaspar. It offers English classes and training for all ages. WHAT THEY OFFER: (1) English classes for kids — after-school and weekend programs. (2) English classes for teens — exam prep (Cambridge, Trinity), school support. (3) English classes for adults — general English, business English, conversation. (4) Kids English Club (KEC) — 100% English summer camps in Madrid. Activities include sports, arts, science, all in English. (5) Professional English training — in-company classes for businesses. (6) Business Basics for Kids — entrepreneurship program for children in English. LOCATIONS: (1) C/ Mundillo 3, 28029 Madrid (main office). (2) Calle Amalia 9, Madrid (academy). Phone: 931 521 43. DIRECTOR: Rebecca Gaspar — experienced in marketing, financial management, and English language training. University of Pennsylvania graduate. LinkedIn: linkedin.com/in/gasparrebecca. Described as 'Gaspar English Language Training Consultant.' HISTORY: IberEnglish has been operating in Madrid for several years. Rebecca Gaspar is listed as Director of IberEnglish LP. The company appears to have grown from a small language school into a broader English education provider, including summer camps and business training. THE ESL CAFE CONNECTION: The user mentioned that Rebecca 'bought' ESL Cafe at some point. This likely refers to a Spain-specific ESL resource or community that Rebecca acquired or managed — not Dave's ESL Cafe (the global site, which is still owned by Dave Sperling). The exact details are unclear from public records, but the connection between IberEnglish and Madrid's English teaching community is well-established. STATUS: Active at iberenglish.com. Facebook page active (facebook.com/iberenglish, 5.0 rating). Currently hiring English teachers (native, qualified). TIPS: (1) If you're looking for English classes in Madrid (for yourself or your children), contact IberEnglish directly. (2) If you're an English teacher looking for work, they're hiring — see their 'Become a Teacher' page at iberenglish.com/web/become-a-teacher. (3) The Kids English Club summer camp is popular — book early (March-April for summer). (4) For the ESL Cafe Spain forum (job board for English teachers), see our Dave's ESL Cafe record.

title: FOROCIO — Study-Abroad Travel Agency
summary: Madrid-based incoming travel agency with 20+ years specializing in tailor-made educational group tours across Spain and Europe. Long-time partner of US university study-abroad programs.
content: FOROCIO is one of Madrid's longest-running incoming travel agencies focused exclusively on educational and study-abroad group travel. Founded over 20 years ago and led by Frank (frank@forocio.com), the company designs custom itineraries for university cohorts, faculty-led programs, and cultural group tours throughout Spain and the rest of Europe. They are a regular exhibitor at NAFSA and partner with US institutions including Boston University's Madrid Internship Program, Adelante Abroad, and UniSpain. Their services span accommodation booking, motor coach rental, multilingual guided tours, and full logistical support for groups of 20-100+ students. FOROCIO fills a specific niche that no other Madrid Insider resource covers: they are not a language school, not a relocation agency, and not a general tour operator — they are a B2B partner for university study-abroad offices that need a trusted local ground operator.

sections.heading: What FOROCIO actually does
sections.body: FOROCIO is a ground operator for study-abroad programs — they handle logistics (housing, transport, excursions) for university groups visiting Madrid and Spain. They are not a school, not a tour operator for individual travelers, and not a relocation service.

items.title: Accommodation
items.body: Hotels, hostels, homestays, and student residences across Madrid and other Spanish cities. Long-standing relationships with student-friendly properties.

items.title: Ground transport
items.body: Private motor coaches for in-city and inter-city travel, airport transfers, and multi-destination Spain/Europe itineraries.

items.title: Guided tours
items.body: Multilingual licensed guides for museums, historical sites, and themed routes (Game of Thrones locations, Camino de Santiago, etc.).

items.title: Excursions
items.body: Day and multi-day trips to Toledo, Segovia, El Escorial, Andalusia, Portugal, France, and Morocco.

sections.heading: Is FOROCIO right for you?
do: Use FOROCIO if you are a university study-abroad office planning a group program in Spain
Contact them 6-12 months ahead for custom itineraries during peak spring and summer terms
Ask about combining Madrid with Seville, Granada, and Barcelona in a single itinerary
dont: Do not contact them for individual travel planning — they only work with groups
Do not confuse them with a Spanish language school — they do not teach Spanish
Do not expect walk-up service — all engagements are pre-booked via email

sections.heading: Historical context
sections.body: FOROCIO was already active during the original Madrid Insider era (2014) and is referenced in old Wayback Machine snapshots of the site. They are one of the few Madrid ecosystem operators that has been continuously active for 20+ years and is still independent.

title: Accent Global Learning — Madrid Study Center
summary: US-college-facing study-abroad program provider with a dedicated Madrid Study Center in Malasaña (Calle de Velarde 3). Designs custom semester and short-term programs for American universities.
content: Accent Global Learning is a study-abroad program provider headquartered in the United States with a Madrid Study Center located in Malasaña at Calle de Velarde 3, just steps from Plaza del Dos de Mayo. Unlike FOROCIO (which is a ground operator handling logistics), Accent designs the academic program itself — they work with US colleges and universities to develop custom semester, summer, and faculty-led programs that combine US-accredited coursework with immersive Madrid-based learning. Their Madrid center includes classrooms, study spaces, and student support staff. They are particularly strong in humanities, social sciences, and Spanish language/culture programming. Accent is a regular participant in NAFSA, Forum on Education Abroad, and other study-abroad professional conferences. Their Instagram (@accentglobal_madrid) and Facebook page document ongoing student activities and center events.

sections.heading: Accent vs FOROCIO — what's the difference?
sections.body: Both serve the US study-abroad market in Madrid, but they operate at different layers of the stack. Accent designs and delivers the academic program (coursework, faculty, classrooms). FOROCIO handles ground logistics (housing, transport, excursions). Many universities use both — Accent for academics, FOROCIO for travel.

items.title: Custom semester programs
items.body: Tailored 15-week programs combining Accent-designed courses with local university enrollment and internships.

items.title: Summer sessions
items.body: 6-8 week intensive programs popular with US undergraduates. Often include regional travel components.

items.title: Faculty-led short programs
items.body: 1-4 week programs where a US professor travels with their class and Accent handles all Madrid-side logistics and guest lectures.

items.title: Internship placements
items.body: Madrid-based internships in fields ranging from art history to international business, with placement support and academic credit.

items.title: Student life support
items.body: On-site staff, orientation, cultural activities, 24/7 emergency support, and homestay or apartment housing coordination.

sections.heading: Not for individual students
sections.body: Accent does not enroll individual students directly. You must be participating through a partner US university to attend an Accent program. If you are an individual student looking for a Madrid study-abroad experience, see Universities in Madrid or Spanish Language Schools instead.

title: American Women's Club of Madrid (AWC)
